systems which have been conducted on a variety of other crop as well (Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016; Kowalczyk and Cupial, 2020; Longo et al., 2017 and Tricase et al., 2018). As per most findings, organic cultivation is more ecological friendly than conventional cultivation due to its lower consumption of fossil fuels and greenhouse gas emissions, which contribute less to environment (Reganold and Wachter, 2016).

In terms of potato cultivation, a growing number of LCA studies on potato farming have been presented in recent decades, the majority of which were primarily concerned with the conventional production systems (Kowalczyk, 2019; Kumar et al., 2022 and Roos et al., 2010), meanwhile few of the studies compared conventional and organic potato cultivation crop production using the LCA method (Selvaraj et al., 2021 and Timpanaro et al., 2021). However, only a few studies have covered organic production SO far, such as (Mattsson and Wallén, 2003;

Pacifico and Paris, 2016 and Singh, 2022). The majority of investigations have been conducted in European nations, and their findings demonstrated that the emissions that caused eutrophication. acidification and global warming were mostly caused by agricultural production, manufacturing of packing materials, and household activities. However, there is a lack of comprehensive research on the environmental performance of organic potato production, particularly in relation to Indian climatic conditions, which may reveal different results due to differences in farming practices, weather conditions, transportation requirements, energy sources, waste management practices, socioeconomic factors, mechanization techniques, and SO on. Therefore, it is essential to perform an LCA study specific to the Indian scenario using first hand data. This study seeks to document the Environmental Impacts of organic potato production at Jalandhar district, Punjab, India. The core goals of the study are to: (1) develop an inventory for environment impact analysis of organic potato production, (2) identify major hotspots in life cycle of the crop and (3) analyze each agricultural activity that most influence the natural environment.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
9: <header>Description of the system </header>
The study evaluates the environmental implications of organically grown potatoes in

northern India. The analysis was carried out with foreground data collection from potato fields in the State of Punjab, India. A total of 20 small sized potato fields (less than 5 hectare) were chosen around the district of Jalandhar, Punjab. All chosen farmers were members of the Jalandhar Potato Growers Association (JPGA), which assured accurate data gathering and the same crop variety sown by all farmers. The district has largest share in state's total potato production (Rana et al., 2020). The surveys were conducted through direct interviews using a questionnaire administered to 15 organic potato growers. LCA tool is considered as the main approach to find environment burden associated with potato cultivation (Table 1).
10: <header>System Boundary </header>

Environment burden of crop production arises not only from agricultural' activities, but also from the extraction, manufacturing, and shipping of essential resources like manure, fuel, and seed. All of these factors must be taken into account in order to perform a thorough evaluation. The LCA boundary consist all input and outputs for a selected production system (Fig 3).

collected via field survey and a standardized questionnaire at the farm level during the crop season 2021-22. In order to prevent any errors, data was further examined by agricultural experts and each field operation and input for each system was recorded in terms of resource use and consumption (Table 2).
In the starting of September, primary tillage operation was carried and the field

was allowed for sun drying (4-5 days). Afterward, the second operation was carried out, and it facilitated the soil by destroying weeds, pests and their breading places in order to prepare the land for sowing practice. In the sowing process an average seed rate used by farmer in the study area was 2.5 tonne per hectare. All the selected farmer used conventional potatoes as seed potatoes to cultivate crop in order to avoid the problem of seed-borne disease. Whole seed tubers were planted without cutting and tuber damage is as little as 1%. The consumption of diesel used in agricultural machinery has been taken in this study. For organic crop, the field preparation practices are performed mechanically, whereas sowing and crop management practices (earthing, weeding, organic fertilizing, and pesticide spraying) are carried out either manually or mechanically depending upon the farmer's decision and the infrastructure available. The relevant data has been taken from world leading database Ecoinvent version 3.7. Furthermore, in the fertilizer application solid cow manure is most commonly used
Table 2 Selected Life cycle Inventory for one hectare potato production

Farming Practices|Activity|Org.|Unit|Source Land Preparation|Transformation, from agriculture|10000|m²|Ecoinvent database Land Preparation|Transformation, to agriculture|10000|m²|Ecoinvent database Land Preparation|Tillage, ploughing|1|Times/ha|Ecoinvent database Land Preparation|Tillage, rotary cultivator|1|Times/ha|Ecoinvent database Land Preparation|Diesel|30|Ltr|Field Survey Sowing|Potato seed|2500|kg|Ecoinvent database Sowing|Diesel|16.5|Ltr|Field Survey Sowing|Manure, solid, cattle|21000|kg|Field Survey Sowing|Transport (by tractor and trailer)|1889.8|tkm|Field Survey Irrigation|Water, fresh|1150.6|m3|Field Survey Irrigation|Electricity, low voltage (IN-Northern grid)|142.2|kWh|Field Survey Crop Protection|Water|250|Ltr|Field Survey Crop Protection|Buttermilk, from cow milk|7.5|kg|Field Survey Harvesting|Diesel|30|Ltr|Field Survey

fertilizers in the respective region (Amritpal and Sidhu, 2015 and Rana et al., 2020). The well-decomposed solid cattle manure, which was mixed with the soil during the last ploughing. The use of organic fertilizer not only prevent the outflow of pollutants into water bodies, but also provides a supply of rich nutrients to potato fields and resources recycling for livestock farming (Manickam et al., 2012). A mean value of 20 tons per hectare along with a mean distance of 50 km to transport this manure to potato farms has been taken in this study. Coming to crop protection, a 10 Ltr of sour buttermilk per hectare was used as a pesticide in this region. Additionally, diesel consumption during earthing up/weeding activities has been also taken in the life cycle inventory.

Water consumption for field activities and to hydrate the cultivation system were recorded according to irrigation type (i.e., groundwater) and water intake. Potatoes need irrigation at frequent interval, depending on the soil and climatic circumstances. In organic cultivation of potatoes generally, three to five irrigations are sufficient, including 1-2 mild irrigations following pre-sowing irrigation. All the selected farmers used electricity as a resource to run the water pumps which was operated on an average for ten hours for doing one irrigation and there were total of four irrigations has been recorded in 1 hectare of field. So, a mean electricity consumption has been taken in the study from all the selected farms. The consumption of diesel by potato harvester (digger) has been also taken in this study. In order to record precise data collection, it was also ensured that all the selected farms used same powered tractor and implement machinery.

collected via field survey and a standardized questionnaire at the farm level during the crop season 2021-22. In order to prevent any errors, data was further examined by agricultural experts and each field operation and input for each system was recorded in terms of resource use and consumption (Table 2).
In the starting of September, primary tillage operation was carried and the field

was allowed for sun drying (4-5 days). Afterward, the second operation was carried out, and it facilitated the soil by destroying weeds, pests and their breading places in order to prepare the land for sowing practice. In the sowing process an average seed rate used by farmer in the study area was 2.5 tonne per hectare. All the selected farmer used conventional potatoes as seed potatoes to cultivate crop in order to avoid the problem of seed-borne disease. Whole seed tubers were planted without cutting and tuber damage is as little as 1%. The consumption of diesel used in agricultural machinery has been taken in this study. For organic crop, the field preparation practices are performed mechanically, whereas sowing and crop management practices (earthing, weeding, organic fertilizing, and pesticide spraying) are carried out either manually or mechanically depending upon the farmer's decision and the infrastructure available. The relevant data has been taken from world leading database Ecoinvent version 3.7. Furthermore, in the fertilizer application solid cow manure is most commonly used
Table 2 Selected Life cycle Inventory for one hectare potato production

Farming Practices|Activity|Org.|Unit|Source Land Preparation|Transformation, from agriculture|10000|m²|Ecoinvent database Land Preparation|Transformation, to agriculture|10000|m²|Ecoinvent database Land Preparation|Tillage, ploughing|1|Times/ha|Ecoinvent database Land Preparation|Tillage, rotary cultivator|1|Times/ha|Ecoinvent database Land Preparation|Diesel|30|Ltr|Field Survey Sowing|Potato seed|2500|kg|Ecoinvent database Sowing|Diesel|16.5|Ltr|Field Survey Sowing|Manure, solid, cattle|21000|kg|Field Survey Sowing|Transport (by tractor and trailer)|1889.8|tkm|Field Survey Irrigation|Water, fresh|1150.6|m3|Field Survey Irrigation|Electricity, low voltage (IN-Northern grid)|142.2|kWh|Field Survey Crop Protection|Water|250|Ltr|Field Survey Crop Protection|Buttermilk, from cow milk|7.5|kg|Field Survey Harvesting|Diesel|30|Ltr|Field Survey

fertilizers in the respective region (Amritpal and Sidhu, 2015 and Rana et al., 2020). The well-decomposed solid cattle manure, which was mixed with the soil during the last ploughing. The use of organic fertilizer not only prevent the outflow of pollutants into water bodies, but also provides a supply of rich nutrients to potato fields and resources recycling for livestock farming (Manickam et al., 2012). A mean value of 20 tons per hectare along with a mean distance of 50 km to transport this manure to potato farms has been taken in this study. Coming to crop protection, a 10 Ltr of sour buttermilk per hectare was used as a pesticide in this region. Additionally, diesel consumption during earthing up/weeding activities has been also taken in the life cycle inventory.

Water consumption for field activities and to hydrate the cultivation system were recorded according to irrigation type (i.e., groundwater) and water intake. Potatoes need irrigation at frequent interval, depending on the soil and climatic circumstances. In organic cultivation of potatoes generally, three to five irrigations are sufficient, including 1-2 mild irrigations following pre-sowing irrigation. All the selected farmers used electricity as a resource to run the water pumps which was operated on an average for ten hours for doing one irrigation and there were total of four irrigations has been recorded in 1 hectare of field. So, a mean electricity consumption has been taken in the study from all the selected farms. The consumption of diesel by potato harvester (digger) has been also taken in this study. In order to record precise data collection, it was also ensured that all the selected farms used same powered tractor and implement machinery.

Since the life cycle inventory data was taken from twenty organic potato farms
SO an average value of the inventory data has been taken.

<list>The geographic location is Jalandhar, Punjab. 
Water which is used for irrigation is 10% rainwater and 90% groundwater. 
The diesel consumption which is used in the inventory is the total diesel burnt in the machinery used in agriculture. 
The mean distance of transportation is taken to be 50 kms for carrying manure to the farm from its source.</list>
18: <header>Life Cycle Impact Assessment </header>

One of the most popular software used for LCA is SimaPro because of its reliability and close integration with authoritative and trustworthy data sources like Ecoinvent Agribalyse, and Agri-footprint , encompass with several datasets related to agriculture and allied industries (Iswara et al., 2020) So Sima pro version 9.0.0 was used in this study to model the LCA of one hectare organic potato production. A life cycle assessment was conducted at midpoint characterization level by employing CML 2000 baseline V2.05, which is one of the most commonly used indicator method in LCA analysis (Abeliotis et al., 2016). The study mainly focused on the characterization and normalized results to present the relevant findings. The findings of the results help us to make practical judgements about how emissions influence the ecosystem, health and resources.
19: <header>RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>

The results and discussion section covers three sub-sections where the first one highlights the Characterization results, the next one presents the Normalization results with respect to involved farming practices and the last section covers the substance contribution to key impact categories.
20: <header>Characterization results and discussion </header>

It is apparent from Fig. 4 that the sowing practice followed by irrigation practice carry the highest impact out of all chosen farming practices across 10 impact categories. When the most influential impact indicators are considered, sowing, irrigation, field preparation, and organic fertilization contribute 33.97 %, 20.42 %, 17.67 %, and 16.95 %, respectively, to the 'global warming' impact.

Fig. 4 Characterization results w.r.t to different farming practices of organic potato cultivation

Based on the characterization results, all the impact indicators have been discussed (Table 3). The Global Warming Potential (GWP 100) was established; this primarily denotes the amount of energy absorbed upon the emissions of a certain gas in relation to the amount of energy absorbed following the emission of one tonne of CO 2 gas (Masset et al., 2014). In the current study, the largest source of greenhouse gases is showing, which accounts for 423.42 kg CO 2 eq (Table 3) In a similar study conducted by Pishgar-Komleh et al., (2012) the amount of GHG emission was reported as 992.88 CO 2 eq. per ha. The dominant contribution attributes to resource utilized in seed material and consumption of diesel during planting. Moreover, the use of seed potato for the current study was from conventional production, hence, the seed related emissions can further be reduced by selected organically grown potato seed (which was currently unavailable to us). By adopting General precautions against pests and diseases, such as crop rotation, use of tolerant or resistant varieties, use of healthy and certified organic seed tubers (Goffart et al., 2022) as well by cultivate with sufficient distance from other potato plots to avoid viral infection and blight (Kreuze et al., 2020) can reduce the problems arising with
Table 3. Characterization values w.r.t to farming practices of organic potato cultivation
organic seed which further lead to weakens environment burden.

Additionally, the practice of sowing adds to acidification, eutrophication, terrestrial ecotoxicity, and photochemical oxidation with a share of 74.47%, 45.27%, 27.53%, and 29.34% respectively. The influence of these impact categories might be due to usage of Cow dung manure during the time of sowing. The cattle manure application can significantly increase CH emissions in soil which leads to toxicity in soil as well as the water bodies (Kim et al., 2014).

Next major contributor is irrigation practice with a share of more than twenty five percent in abiotic depletion (25.56%), Ozone layer depletion (37%), freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity (34.60%) and marine aquatic ecotoxicity (36.88%). This can be attribute to high usage of groundwater consumption and electricity consumption to run the water pump (Timpanaro et al., 2021). The use of drip irrigation can reduce this particular impact by a noticeable amount however, it is still not in much practice in the region under study.

The other phases include organic fertilization, field preparation, harvesting and crop protection are generally proportionate to resource usage (Kumar et al., 2022); for example, organic fertilization impact was shown to be greater due to high amount of organic manure usage and diesel consumption involved in their transportation to farm. Overall, it would be fair to state that the sowing & irrigation phase lends a major portion of contribution to the environmental.

26: <header>Normalization result and discussion </header>
It was also important to normalize the results in order to have a better understanding of the severity of the environment consequences related with the potato cultivation (Prasara and Gheewala,

2016). After normalization the impact category indicators all have the same unit, which makes it easier to compare them. The Fig 5 shows that 'marine aquatic ecotoxicity' contributed the largest to the overall environment; the fresh water aquatic ecotoxicity, eutrophication, and acidification also holds substantial contributions, but the impact of other categories on the ecosystem were almost negligible. The higher contribution by marine aquatic ecotoxicity is primarily attributed to the irrigation, organic fertilization and field preparation stages, due to the soil toxicants leaching into the groundwater or getting transferred with sediments by runoff, thus polluting freshwater aquatic ecosystems, and promoting eutrophication (Tuomisto et al., 2012). The categories namely acidification and eutrophication were caused by sowing process due to fossil fuel usage in mechanical agitation i.e., tractor ploughing, and rotary cultivator etc. of soil (Khanali et al., 2020). Nevertheless, such percentage can be brought down by adopting more efficient sowing mechanisms or by further optimizing the

design parameters of the planting equipment for reduced load on the tractor. However, such an analysis is beyond the scope of the current study.

30: <header>CONCLUSION </header>

The current study enlists the most crucial environment impact indicators and also evaluates their contribution towards the ecological footprint in the production of one hectare of organic potatoes. It is found that the sowing practice followed by irrigation practice emit the highest impact out of all chosen agricultural practices across all the major impact categories. Furthermore, it is suggested that at the farmers end, adoption of efficient sowing mechanism using advanced irrigation technologies like drip irrigation and developing infrastructure for organic manure production can effectively reduce the impact of there of hotspots. Also, at the govt's end, the development of renewable energy sources can counter act the negative impacts of conventional agriculture.

0: <title>EFFECT OF CLIMATE VARIABILITY ON POTATO YIELD UNDER DIFFERENT AGROCLIMATIC ZONES OF PUNJAB </title>
Dilpreet Singh and Sarabjot Kaur Sandhu*

ABSTRACT: A study was conducted to determine the role of meteorological parameters on year to year variability in potato yield in two different agroclimatic zones of Punjab viz. Central Zone (Ludhiana) and Western Zone (Bathinda) using last 17 years (2003-04 to 2019-20) data. The meteorological data of different months from October to January was used to conduct this study. The meteorological data of October and November month did not show any significance for locations. Whereas, meteorological data of December and January month significantly influenced potato yield at both locations. Potato yield showed highly significant positive correlation with minimum temperature and evening relative humidity for both locations whereas yield showed significant negative correlation with sunshine hours at Ludhiana. After studying the deviation of meteorological parameters from respective normals during highest and lowest yield years and the correlation of meteorological parameters with potato yield it can be concluded that in the Punjab minimum temperature along with sunshine hours play a major role in determining the potato yield. Regression model based on maximum and minimum temperature, morning relative humidity and sunshine hours gave good results. Validation of the regression models for prediction of potato yield proved the efficiency of the targeted predictions at both locations.
KEYWORDS: Potato, Agroclimatic Zones, Meteorological Parameters, Regression Models, Validation

It belongs to the genus Solanum of the Solanaceae family. It was first introduced in the Europe by the Spanish conquerors in the end of 16th century. In India, it was introduced in early 17th century by Portuguese traders. At world level, potato is the third- largest food crop after wheat and rice and is cultivated in numerous countries, including China, India and Iran (Arab et al., 2012). Total area under potato in India is ~2.16 million ha (m ha) with the production of 51.3 million tonnes (mt) and average productivity of 23.8 t ha-Superscript(1) far behind China (Choudhary et al., 2021). der Zaag, 1990; Paul et al., 2016). Various studies have projected a 10-40 per cent loss in potato production in India by 2080-2100 due to climate change unless farmers adapt to climate change (IPCC, 2007). Its growing duration in the IGP is projected to decrease due to climate change. The effect of climate change on potato production in India has been studied by Singh et al. (2009) and they revealed that under the warming scenarios, potato productivity is expected to decline in all potato growing states of India. The radiation use efficiency (RUE) is suppressed under high temperatures (Allen and Scott, 1992; Rajpoot et al., 2021). It requires cool night temperature to induce tuberization (Ewing, 1997). Potato is very sensitive to frost. Severe damage of foliage occurs when temperature falls below 0°C (Hijmans et al., 2003). Temperature less than 1°C for more than 4-5 hours can cause 50 per cent loss of foliage even in one night. Frost causes burning and leaf withering. Prolonged cold can cause a purplish discolouration of edges of leaflets. The frozen part of tuber become soft and blackens from inside. Frost can cause high reduction in yield. However, the reduction of yield depends on stage of crop when frost occurs. If frost occurs at 80-90 days after planting then 10-15 per cent yield loss occur while at 50-60 days after planting then 30-40 per cent yield loss occur. In Punjab, frost is the very dangerous for potato growth and yield. When planting is delayed beyond third week of October then severe damage can occur due to frost. This can be avoided by timely planting of crop. Frost can cause reduction of leaf area and can cause decrease in photosynthesis and yield. Potato is well known for its specific temperature and day length requirement for tuber formation as well as flowering, SO it becomes the most vulnerable crop for climate change. As potato is the major food crop at global scale, thus

evaluating the effect of climate change on its productivity is the need of hour, SO keeping this in view, the effect of climate variability on potato yield under two agro-climatic zones of Punjab have been analyzed and discussed in this paper.

Prolonged cold can cause a purplish discolouration of edges of leaflets. The frozen part of tuber become soft and blackens from inside. Frost can cause high reduction in yield. However, the reduction of yield depends on stage of crop when frost occurs. If frost occurs at 80-90 days after planting then 10-15 per cent yield loss occur while at 50-60 days after planting then 30-40 per cent yield loss occur. In Punjab, frost is the very dangerous for potato growth and yield. When planting is delayed beyond third week of October then severe damage can occur due to frost. This can be avoided by timely planting of crop. Frost can cause reduction of leaf area and can cause decrease in photosynthesis and yield. Potato is well known for its specific temperature and day length requirement for tuber formation as well as flowering, SO it becomes the most vulnerable crop for climate change. As potato is the major food crop at global scale, thus

evaluating the effect of climate change on its productivity is the need of hour, SO keeping this in view, the effect of climate variability on potato yield under two agro-climatic zones of Punjab have been analyzed and discussed in this paper.
5: <header>MATERIALS AND METHODS </header>

The impact of meteorological parameters on potato yield was analysed by conducting analysis of historical data. The historical data (2003-04 to 2019-20) of potato yield was collected for Ludhiana and Bathinda from "Statistical Abstracts of Punjab". The data of different meteorological parameters viz. temperature, relative humidity, rainfall, rainy days, sunshine hours for both locations was collected from Department of Climate Change and Agricultural Meteorology. The normal weekly meteorological data of Ludhiana was calculated by using data of 1970-2017 and for Bathinda was calculated by using data of 1977-2015. The potato yield data was analyzed and year with highest and lowest yield were identified. The deviations of different parameters were calculated for Ludhiana and Bathinda for highest and lowest yield year. Correlation and regression analysis was conducted to study the relation between potato yield and different meteorological parameters. The validation of developed regression models for Ludhiana and Bathinda was done by using data of 2004-05 and 2009- 10 and 2005-06 and 2018-19 (that was not used in development of model) respectively. These years' data was used for predicting the potato yield.
6: <header>Meteorological parameters and potato yield at Ludhiana </header>
The perusal of weekly maximum and minimum temperature during highest (2019-20) and lowest (2005-06) potato yield

year at Ludhiana revealed that maximum temperature was mostly below normal during highest yield year. The deviations of temperatures were calculated for both highest and lowest yield year and it was observed that during 2019-20 i.e. highest yield year, deviation of maximum temperature was in range of -7.9°C to 0.0°C as shown in Table 1. This data showed that during crop duration period in 2019-20, maximum temperature was not above normal even for a single week. Whereas, for lowest yield year deviation of maximum temperature was in range of -0.2°C to +3.7°C. Out of 18 weeks, 14 weeks recorded above normal maximum temperature and resulted in lower potato yield. Comparison of weekly minimum temperature, during these two years indicate that during highest yield year only 3 weeks out of 18 weeks observed below normal minimum temperature and

on the other hand during lowest potato yield year 11 weeks recorded below normal temperature during crop duration period. Similarly, Singh et al., (2010) revealed that lower yield is most common problem in areas where night temperatures fall below optimum during winter season.

The analysis of rainfall and sunshine hours prevailed during highest and lowest potato yield year is presented in Table 2. The highest yield year recorded 68.7 mm above normal rainfall whereas lowest yield year recorded 36.3 mm below normal rainfall. The comparison of sunshine hours indicate that lower sunshine hours favoured potato yield as more sunshine hours lead to more frost days. So, it was observed that, highest yield year recorded very low sunshine hours than normal as compared to lowest yield year which recorded more sunshine hours than highest
Table 1. Weekly maximum and minimum temperature deviation from normal during highest and lowest potato yield year at Ludhiana
Where, Tmax: Maximum Temperature Tmin Minimum Temperature H: Highest yield year L: Lowest yield year
Table 2. Weekly rainfall and sunshine hour's deviation from normal during highest and lowest potato yield year at Ludhiana

yield year. Comparison of the number of frost days between highest and lowest potato yield years at Ludhiana is shown in Fig. 1. In the year 2005-06, highest number of frost days (17 and 12 days) was recorded in month of December and January respectively, whereas in 2019-20 very few days (1 and 4) observed frost during December and January. Hijmans et al., (2003) also revealed that potato is highly sensitive to frost and severe damage may occur when temperature drops below 0°C.

revealed that during highest yield year (2003-04), deviation of maximum temperature was in range of -5.5°C to +1.3°C as shown in Table 3. Whereas, for lowest yield year deviation of maximum temperature was in range of -0.2°C to +1.4°C. The comparison of weekly minimum temperature, during these
Table 3. Weekly maximum and minimum temperature deviation from normal during highest and lowest potato yield year at Bathinda

two years indicate that during highest yield year deviation of minimum temperature was in range of -6.6°C to +2.1°C and on the other hand during lowest potato yield year, range of deviation was -2.9°C to +2.5°C. Dua et al. (2018) revealed that increase in temperature can lower potato yield by 10-11.4 per cent.

The analysis of rainfall received during highest and lowest potato yield year is presented in Table 4. The highest yield year recorded 1.1 mm below normal rainfall whereas lowest yield year recorded 22.2 mm below normal rainfall. The Comparison of the number of frost days between highest and lowest potato yield years at Bathinda is shown in Fig. 1. 14: <header>Meteorological parameters and potato yield at Ludhiana </header>
The perusal of weekly maximum and minimum temperature during highest (2019-20) and lowest (2005-06) potato yield

year at Ludhiana revealed that maximum temperature was mostly below normal during highest yield year. The deviations of temperatures were calculated for both highest and lowest yield year and it was observed that during 2019-20 i.e. highest yield year, deviation of maximum temperature was in range of -7.9°C to 0.0°C as shown in Table 1. This data showed that during crop duration period in 2019-20, maximum temperature was not above normal even for a single week. Whereas, for lowest yield year deviation of maximum temperature was in range of -0.2°C to +3.7°C. Out of 18 weeks, 14 weeks recorded above normal maximum temperature and resulted in lower potato yield. Comparison of weekly minimum temperature, during these two years indicate that during highest yield year only 3 weeks out of 18 weeks observed below normal minimum temperature and

on the other hand during lowest potato yield year 11 weeks recorded below normal temperature during crop duration period. Similarly, Singh et al., (2010) revealed that lower yield is most common problem in areas where night temperatures fall below optimum during winter season.

The analysis of rainfall and sunshine hours prevailed during highest and lowest potato yield year is presented in Table 2. The highest yield year recorded 68.7 mm above normal rainfall whereas lowest yield year recorded 36.3 mm below normal rainfall. The comparison of sunshine hours indicate that lower sunshine hours favoured potato yield as more sunshine hours lead to more frost days. So, it was observed that, highest yield year recorded very low sunshine hours than normal as compared to lowest yield year which recorded more sunshine hours than highest

18: <header>Correlation Analysis </header>

The pooled correlation coefficients between potato yield and different meteorological parameters were calculated for Ludhiana and Bathinda on the basis of potato yield and monthly data of different meteorological parameters prevailed during crop duration period as presented in Table 1. For Ludhiana, potato yield and meteorological parameters data of 2004-05 and 2009-10 years was not used for correlation and regression analysis as these years data was used for model validation.

Similarly, for Bathinda, potato yield and meteorological parameters data of 2005- 06 and 2018-19 years were not used for correlation and regression analysis as these years' data was used for model validation. Among this correlation coefficient analysis, meteorological data of October month did not show any significance for both locations.
Table 4. Weekly rainfall deviation from normal during highest and lowest potato yield year at Bathinda

Whereas, meteorological data of December and January month significantly influenced potato yield at both locations. Potato yield showed highly significant positive correlation with minimum temperature and evening relative humidity whereas yield showed significant negative correlation with sunshine hours at Ludhiana.

21: <header>Development of a regression model </header>

On the basis of different correlation coefficients and relationships of potato yield with different meteorological parameters most influencing meteorological parameters were identified from different months. These meteorological parameters were from crop duration period. After analyzing number of regression equations best fit equation with significant R2-value was selected as a model

for forewarning potato yield at Ludhiana and Bathinda. For Ludhiana, selected regression model includes maximum and minimum temperature, morning and evening relative humidity, rainfall and sunshine hours as independent variables and potato yield as dependent variable as dependent variable. The regression models for Ludhiana and Bathinda are as follows:
22: <header>Where, </header>
Y= Potato Yield (Kg/ha) Tmax: Maximum Temperature (C) Tmin: Minimum Temperature (C) RHm: Morning Relative Humidity (%) RHe: Evening Relative Humidity(%) RF: Rainfall (mm) SSh: Sunshine Hours (hrs)
23: <header>R2: Coefficient of Determination </header>

This model indicates that potato yield at Ludhiana as well as Bathinda is highly influenced by meteorological parameters. The significant R2-value of 0.54 indicates that 54 per cent variability in potato yield at Ludhiana was due to different meteorological parameters. Similarly, for Bathinda district, R2-value of 0.64 indicates that 64 per cent variability in potato yield at Bathinda was due to different meteorological parameters (Table 5).

26: <header>CONCLUSION </header>

The analysis of meteorological data for both locations indicated that deviations were comparatively less during high yield year as compared to lowest yield years. Number of frost days was significantly higher during lowest yield years. Correlation coefficients between different meteorological parameters and potato yield showed that minimum temperature, morning relative humidity and sunshine hours during December and January period significantly influenced potato yield at Ludhiana and Bathinda. Regression equation between different meteorological parameters and potato yield indicated that potato yield

is highly dependent on weather conditions of December and January. Significant R2 values explained that more than 54 and 64 per cent variability in potato yield at Ludhiana and Bathinda respectively, was due to different meteorological parameters.

0: <title>FORECASTING OF POTATO PRODUCTION IN HIMACHAL PRADESH USING ARIMA MODEL </title>
Sapna Bhardwaj1* and Banti Kumar2

ABSTRACT: This study focuses on the application of the ARIMA time series model to forecast the future potato production in Himachal Pradesh based on past data from 1991-92 to 2019-20. The stationarity of the data has been checked using the augmented dickey Fuller test which showed that the data was non-stationary. Therefore, differencing up to the first order has been done to make it stationary. Based on various model selection criteria, the best-fitted model was ARIMA (0, 1, 1) which has been used for the forecast of the next five years i.e., from 2020-2024. From the forecasted figure, potato production will go from 29612.36 tons to 22543.08 tons in 2024 showing an increasing trend.
KEYWORDS: ARIMA, Stationarity, Forecasting, Time Series data, Potato production
1: <header>INTRODUCTION </header>

Potato plays a vital role in the agricultural economy. India is the 2nd largest producer of potato in the world after China, accounting for ~12% of global potato production (Choudhary et al., 2021). It is economically important not only for its contribution to the livelihood of farmers but also for its dominance in the agricultural consumption basket of households (Paul et al., 2016). China is the biggest producer of potatoes worldwide, with about 1/3rd of the world's potatoes produced in China and India. According to FAO estimates, in 2020, over 359 million tons (mt) of potatoes were produced worldwide, an increase from a production volume of 333.6 mt in 2010. Potato (Solanum tuberosum) is the 3rd top most consumed crop worldwide after rice and wheat (International Potato Center, 2013).
In Indian agricultural GDP, the contribution of potatoes from the unit area of cultivable land is about 3.7 times higher than rice and 5.4 times higher than wheat

(Indian Horticulture, 2019). Some popular potato varieties have been developed by ICAR-Central Potato Research Institute for farmers which cover almost 95 % of the total area under potatoes. India produced approximately 45.87 mt of potato annually during the triennium 2014-17 and contributed ~57,512 crores annually to the Gross Value Added (GVA) at current prices (Indian Horticulture, 2019). The economy of Himachal Pradesh is largely dependent on agriculture. About 12.73 % of the total gross state domestic product (GSDP) is generated through agricultural and allied sectors (Economics Survey of Himachal Pradesh, 2019-20). So, it is an essential sector for the sustained growth of the state economy. Although rice, wheat, and maize are major agricultural crops grown in the state yet, the potato is also one of the important commercial crops. Out of total vegetable production in the country, the major share goes to potatoes (28.9 %). Out of the total 17.22 lakh tons production of vegetables in the state, potato accounts for 1.87 lakh
tons i.e. 10.85 % which is the largest among all other vegetables (Economics Survey of Himachal Pradesh, 2019-20).

In this research paper, forecasting of potato production has been done for the next five years (2020 to 2024) for Himachal Pradesh. In recent times, forecasting methods have been increasingly applied by researchers. Some popular forecasting analysis methods are exponential smoothing methods, the Box-Jenkins ARIMA model, the Grey forecasting model, and others (Sahinli, 2020). Time series analysis for forecasting may be regarded as a useful practice to develop an appropriate econometric model for estimating future behavior based on previously observed behaviour (Ray and Bhattacharyya, 2020).

ARIMA (p, d, q) models are created by first taking the differences of the data from d degree for the stabilization process and then adding the ARMA (p, q) model. Hossain and Faruq (2016) used a time series model to find out the patterns in the past and utilize that information to forecast the future values of potato production in Bangladesh. They selected Box-Jenkins ARIMA (0,2,1) model for forecasting the potato production for the whole of Bangladesh. Jubair et al. (2019) carried out horizontal variability of some soil properties in Wasit governorate Iraq by using time series analysis. Mishra et al. (2021) did an estimation of fish production in India using ARIMA, Holt's Linear, BATS, and TBATS Models. Jamuna et al. (2021) analyzed the price of potatoes through the time series analysis using a price forecasting system that is implemented based on ARIMA Model. The potato price at one main market for each state was examined using the ARIMA model based on monthly average data obtained from Karnataka, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Rajasthan, and Maharashtra. Kumar
and Baishya (2020) worked on forecasting of potato prices in India through the ARIMA model. Kumar et al. (2019) also used time series modeling to forecast the oilseed production in India.

ARIMA is also called as Box-Jenkins model because it was proposed by George Box and Gwilym Jenkins in 1976. Since, the ARIMA model employs non-zero autocorrelation between the subsequent values of the time series data, it was chosen for predicting in the current study. The present work has been taken with the following objectives:

<list>(i) To investigate the past trend in potato production in Himachal Pradesh. 
(ii) To develop an ARIMA model for potato production in Himachal Pradesh. 
(iii) To forecast the future potato production for the next 5 years i.e. from 2019 to 2020.</list>

6: <header>MATERIALS AND METHODS </header>

Time series data on potato production in Himachal Pradesh for the last 30 years, i.e., from 1991-92 to 2019-20 were used for the present study. Data were procured from Statistical Yearbooks of Himachal Pradesh published by the Government of H.P.
7: <header>Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average Model: </header>

Three elements make up an ARIMA model: Moving Average (MA), Integration (I), and Autoregression (AR). The variable of interest is regressed on its own lagged values to create the AR model. I display how many times the series has been made stationary by differencing. A stochastic term's current value as well as several previous values are linearly reliant on the output variable that MA represents. A basic ARIMA model is expressed as (p, d, q), where p signifies the

0: <title>Potato production forecasting </title>
order of the moving average process, d the number of times the series has undergone differencing to make it stationary, and q the order of the autoregressive process.

A time series must be stationary in order to be used for forecasting. In the event that the series is not stationary, it must be made stationary by taking the differencing up to the appropriate order. A time series generating process's statistical characteristic that doesn't change over time is called stationarity. If the mean, variance, autocorrelation, etc. remain constant across time, a series is said to be stationary. If the conditions listed below are true for every t, a time series Yt is stationary. E Cov (Y, V(1-k))

To test whether a series is stationary or not, one may apply the unit root test. The unit root test checks whether the given time series has a unit root or not. Augmented Dickey- Fuller (1979) test is prominently used which assumes that the error term in the model is correlated.

Correlogram: It is a visualisation of a time series' time lag against partial and autocorrelation correlations. The underlying behaviour of a time series is examined using the patterns displayed in a correlogram. The basic connection between Xt and, let's say, Xt+h is shown by autocorrelation; this correlation between series has a lag. The autocorrelation function is expressed mathematically as:
P var (y)

Partial autocorrelation is the relationship between two observations after eliminating the linear relationship of every observation that comes between those two observations. Mathematically, it is given as:
The autocorrelation plot in the correlogram helps to decide the order of MA (q) whereas the partial autocorrelation plot helps to identify the order of AR (p).

Ljung-Box Q statistic: It is used to assess whether the observations are independent and arbitrary over time. It is a way to test for the absence of serial autocorrelation, up to a specified lag k. The test determines whether or not the autocorrelations for the errors or residuals are non-zero. The null hypothesis of the Ljung-Box Test is that H: the model does not show a lack of fit. The alternative hypothesis is that H: the model does show a lack of fit. A significant p-value in this test rejects the null hypothesis that the time series data are not autocorrelated. Ljung-Box Q statistic has an asymptotic chi-square distribution.
A Box Jenkins methodology can be applied in three main steps viz., Identification, estimation and model checking, and forecasting.

Identification: To determine if the provided time series is stationary or not, model identification is used. If a stationary model is discovered, forecasting can be done using it. If it isn't, the series must be differentiated in order to make it stationary. An ARIMA model is then given for the differences series after the original series has been substituted by a series of differencing.

Estimation and Model Checking: Diagnostic testing, which is a stage in ARIMA modelling, entails looking at autocorrelation and partial autocorrelation functions for the estimation of residual behaviour. This aids
in determining the situations in which the model is inappropriate. At this point, the estimated models are tested to see if they meet the requirements for single-variable time series.
Forecasting: In this step, the estimated model is used to predict the future values of the time series.

Ljung-Box Q statistic: It is used to assess whether the observations are independent and arbitrary over time. It is a way to test for the absence of serial autocorrelation, up to a specified lag k. The test determines whether or not the autocorrelations for the errors or residuals are non-zero. The null hypothesis of the Ljung-Box Test is that H: the model does not show a lack of fit. The alternative hypothesis is that H: the model does show a lack of fit. A significant p-value in this test rejects the null hypothesis that the time series data are not autocorrelated. Ljung-Box Q statistic has an asymptotic chi-square distribution.
A Box Jenkins methodology can be applied in three main steps viz., Identification, estimation and model checking, and forecasting.

Identification: To determine if the provided time series is stationary or not, model identification is used. If a stationary model is discovered, forecasting can be done using it. If it isn't, the series must be differentiated in order to make it stationary. An ARIMA model is then given for the differences series after the original series has been substituted by a series of differencing.

Estimation and Model Checking: Diagnostic testing, which is a stage in ARIMA modelling, entails looking at autocorrelation and partial autocorrelation functions for the estimation of residual behaviour. This aids
in determining the situations in which the model is inappropriate. At this point, the estimated models are tested to see if they meet the requirements for single-variable time series.
Forecasting: In this step, the estimated model is used to predict the future values of the time series.

Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test for checking stationarity of the data: ADF test is the most widely used test for determining the stationarity of data. The ADF test has the hypothesis that H: the non-stationarity of the data against H1: the data are stationary. The critical value for the rejection of the null hypothesis of the unit root test was not significant as the p-value is (>0.05) at 5 % level of significance. The results showed that the data regarding potato production were non-stationary and could be used for forecasting purposes.

11: <header>CONCLUSION </header>

In this study, ARIMA (0, 1, 1) model was developed and the validity of the forecasted values can be checked when the data for the lead periods become available. The correctness of the anticipated values may be verified once the data for the lead periods are available since an ARIMA (0, 1, 1) model was used in this study. Analysis of the performance of potato production from preceding 1991-92 to 2019-20 gives us the ARIMA (0, 1, 1) model which helps us in forecasting the future values of potato production. 0: <title>BIODIVERSITY AND IDENTIFICATION OF POTATO WHITE GRUBS IN HIGH HILLS OF HIMACHAL PRADESH </title>
Anshul Thakur', RS Chandel, Karthik Ramappa and Niraj Guleria¹

ABSTRACT: The most challenging insect-pests of the potato crop in Himachal Pradesh are white grubs. Their diversity tends to fluctuate, depending on altitude, geography, ecosystems, and other environmental variables. The survey on white grubs of potato was carried out in 19 locations from five districts of Himachal Pradesh during 2021-22. The altitude of these locations varied from 1378-3279m amsl. In total, 13 species of white grubs were collected feeding on roots and tubers of potato crop. Out of 13 species, 8 species have been identified as Brahmina crinicollis Burmeister, Brahmina coriacea (Hope), Brahmina flavosericea Brenske, Holotrichia longipennis Blanchard, Anomala rufiventris Redtenbacher, Melolontha furcicauda Ancey, Melolantha cuprescens Blanchard and Polyphylla sikkimensis Brenseke. We couldn't rear four species till adult emergence and hence this species remained unidentified. The raster patterns of all 13 species have been described with suitable pictures.
KEYWORDS: Potato, white grub, raster pattern, distribution
1: <header>INTRODUCTION </header>

Potato is cultivated in almost all states of India under diverse agro-climatic conditions and has wider adaptability. It is a high- volume crop and yields substantially more edible energy, protein and dry matter per unit area and time because of a short vegetative cycle than many other crops (Dhiman et al., 2007). India produces about 7.72 per cent of the world's potato from 7.57 per cent of the total global potato growing area, with productivity levels higher than the world's average (Rana 2011). In Himachal Pradesh, potato occupies an area of 14407 hectares with production of about 1.97 lakh MT (Anonymous 2021).

Insect-pests cause variable and complex problems for potato farmers. India has a great diversity of insect-pests that attack potato. The significance of various insect-pests in potato production varies from region to region. According to Simpson (1977), potato

crop is attacked by more than 100 arthropod pests, and out of these 80 pests have been reported from India (Saxena and Misra 1983). Among different insect-pests, white grubs are more destructive and troublesome insects, impeding potato production especially in hilly states of India (Chandel et al., 2015). White grubs are immature stages of scarab beetles popularly known as chaffer beetles or May-June beetles.

This is a polyphagous pest both in adult and grub stage and inflicts heavy damage on various fruit/forest trees, their nurseries, vegetables, lawns and field crops (Chandel et al., 2010). Being polyphagous they feed on a wide variety of cultivated as well as uncultivated plants (Mehta et al., 2010).
In India, no record exists of any white grub damage to potato tubers until the 1960s. The first record of white grubs causing damage to potato in India is from Himachal Pradesh by Sharma and Bhalla

(1964). They mentioned two species viz., Brahmina coriacea (Hope) and Lachnosterna (=Holotrichia) longipennis Blanchard causing damage to several crops including potato in Himachal Pradesh. The problem of white grubs is quite serious in the entire north- western Himalaya, where potatoes are grown during summer season as a rainfed crop under long day conditions (Misra and Chandel 2003). In Himachal Pradesh, potato cultivation is done on sloppy lands with light and loose-textured soils and high rainfall. The potato fields are normally situated in the vicinity of fruit orchards in entire north- western Himalaya, and the adults of white grubs prefer to feed on fruit trees during night (Chandel et al., 1997), and enter into nearby potato fields where they lay eggs in the soil during May-June (Chandel et al., 1995). These conditions are conducive for development and multiplication of white grubs (Singh et al., 2002). In India, 24 species of white grubs have been recorded to damage potato from the states of Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Jammu & Kashmir, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka and north-eastern hill states. The most widespread and destructive species are B. coriacea, Brahmina flavosericea Brenske, Melolontha indica Hope, Anomala dimidiata (Hope), H. longipennis, Holotrichia sikkimensis (Brenske), Holotrichia seticollis Moser, Phyllognathus dionysius (Fabricius) and Lepidiota mansueta Burmeister (Chandel et al., 2013, Bhatacharyya et al., 2011).
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Field survey for collection of white grubs from potato

The survey on potato white grubs was carried out in 19 locations from five districts, mainly in high hills areas of Himachal Pradesh (Table 1). The survey work continued for about one year, the sampling sites in a field were selected randomly by throwing

a 1 feet2 quadrant over one's shoulder with no two sampling pits overlapping, the data on grub population were recorded by using pit sampling method with each pit consisting of 1 cubic feet area. At each location, at least 20 samples were taken. At each location sampling of soil was done by carefully digging field by shovel and different larval, pupal and adult stages were carefully collected. The collected white grubs were brought to the laboratory in polythene bags along with moist soil. The observations on raster pattern were taken under the stereo zoom microscope model LEICA S9i. The rearing of grubs was done in the white grub laboratory, Department of Entomology, CSK HPKV, Palampur for adult emergence, and their further identification from the relevant sources. In the laboratory, the grubs were sorted out species wise on the basis of their raster pattern. Some of the white grubs were preserved in 70 per cent alcohol and data on their length and breadth were also recorded. After through sorting of larvae, grubs were transferred to individual paper cups/glass jars having soil and FYM mixture (1:1). Potato tubers were given as food for developing larvae, and observations were recorded on their development up to May. 2022. After adult emergence, beetles were killed and sent to NBAIR, Bengaluru for identification.

Potato is cultivated in almost all states of India under diverse agro-climatic conditions and has wider adaptability. crop is attacked by more than 100 arthropod pests, and out of these 80 pests have been reported from India (Saxena and Misra 1983). Among different insect-pests, white grubs are more destructive and troublesome insects, impeding potato production especially in hilly states of India (Chandel et al., 2015). White grubs are immature stages of scarab beetles popularly known as chaffer beetles or May-June beetles.

This is a polyphagous pest both in adult and grub stage and inflicts heavy damage on various fruit/forest trees, their nurseries, vegetables, lawns and field crops (Chandel et al., 2010). Being polyphagous they feed on a wide variety of cultivated as well as uncultivated plants (Mehta et al., 2010).
In India, no record exists of any white grub damage to potato tubers until the 1960s. The first record of white grubs causing damage to potato in India is from Himachal Pradesh by Sharma and Bhalla

(1964). They mentioned two species viz., Brahmina coriacea (Hope) and Lachnosterna (=Holotrichia) longipennis Blanchard causing damage to several crops including potato in Himachal Pradesh. The problem of white grubs is quite serious in the entire north- western Himalaya, where potatoes are grown during summer season as a rainfed crop under long day conditions (Misra and Chandel 2003). In Himachal Pradesh, potato cultivation is done on sloppy lands with light and loose-textured soils and high rainfall. The potato fields are normally situated in the vicinity of fruit orchards in entire north- western Himalaya, and the adults of white grubs prefer to feed on fruit trees during night (Chandel et al., 1997), and enter into nearby potato fields where they lay eggs in the soil during May-June (Chandel et al., 1995). These conditions are conducive for development and multiplication of white grubs (Singh et al., 2002). In India, 24 species of white grubs have been recorded to damage potato from the states of Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Jammu & Kashmir, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka and north-eastern hill states. The most widespread and destructive species are B. coriacea, Brahmina flavosericea Brenske, Melolontha indica Hope, Anomala dimidiata (Hope), H. longipennis, Holotrichia sikkimensis (Brenske), Holotrichia seticollis Moser, Phyllognathus dionysius (Fabricius) and Lepidiota mansueta Burmeister (Chandel et al., 2013, Bhatacharyya et al., 2011).
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Field survey for collection of white grubs from potato

The survey on potato white grubs was carried out in 19 locations from five districts, mainly in high hills areas of Himachal Pradesh (Table 1). The survey work continued for about one year, the sampling sites in a field were selected randomly by throwing

a 1 feet2 quadrant over one's shoulder with no two sampling pits overlapping, the data on grub population were recorded by using pit sampling method with each pit consisting of 1 cubic feet area. At each location, at least 20 samples were taken. At each location sampling of soil was done by carefully digging field by shovel and different larval, pupal and adult stages were carefully collected. The collected white grubs were brought to the laboratory in polythene bags along with moist soil. The observations on raster pattern were taken under the stereo zoom microscope model LEICA S9i. The rearing of grubs was done in the white grub laboratory, Department of Entomology, CSK HPKV, Palampur for adult emergence, and their further identification from the relevant sources. In the laboratory, the grubs were sorted out species wise on the basis of their raster pattern. Some of the white grubs were preserved in 70 per cent alcohol and data on their length and breadth were also recorded. After through sorting of larvae, grubs were transferred to individual paper cups/glass jars having soil and FYM mixture (1:1). Potato tubers were given as food for developing larvae, and observations were recorded on their development up to May. 2022. After adult emergence, beetles were killed and sent to NBAIR, Bengaluru for identification.

6: <header>RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>
The survey on potato white grubs was carried out at 19 different locations in Himachal Pradesh, and 13 species of white grubs were collected as shown in table 1.

In Chamba district, Brahmina crinicollis was found to be the predominant species comprising 100 per cent population at Salooni and 68.18 per cent at Mani. At Shillaroo, Chidgaon, Matiana, Kufri and Fagu in Shimla district, only Brahmina coriacea grubs were

found to cause damage to potato tubers. Maximum population of white grubs was recorded at Shillaroo with a mean of 11.45+1.05 grubs/ft2. At Rohru and Tikkar, B. coriacea comprised 77.38 and 56.20 per cent of total grub count, respectively.
The observations recorded on morphometrics and development of different species is discussed here under.
Brahmina coriacea (Hope): The grubs caused heavy damage to potato ranging from 8.20 to 45.90 per cent in high hills of Shimla, Mandi

and Kullu districts of Himachal Pradesh. In Shimla district, B. coriacea grubs comprised 56.06 to 100.00 per cent at Tikkar, Rohru, Chidgaon, Shillaroo, Matiana, Kufri and Fagu. Chandel et al. (2003) also found B. coriacea to be a common species in Shimla hills of Himachal Pradesh. constituting about 90 per cent of the population. In Thanedhar, Kotgarh, Matiana and Jubbal areas, 100.00 per cent beetles of B. coriacea have been taken in different collections by various workers (Singh and Dogra 1984). In the present study, observations were recorded on grubs and pupae of B. coriacea under laboratory conditions at Palampur. The first instar grubs of B. coriacea were collected during July 2021 from potato fields located in Shillaroo, Matiana, Fagu and Kufri areas. The collected grubs were greyish white in colour, and their body length ranged from 6.2 to 7.3 mm. Mean head capsule width was recorded to be 1.64+0.09 mm (range: 1.59-1.74 mm). The identity of grubs was confirmed based on their raster pattern as described by Pathania et al., (2017). The raster pattern is given in fig. 1, and it is ornamented with two rows of palidia. There are 13 pali in each row. The pali of one side are crossing the corresponding pali from other side in the middle of septula. There is gradual narrowing of septula from posterior to interior direction. The hemate setae are bold and short. The palidium was broad at the base, tapered gradually, with pointed tip. The hemate setae seems to be fitted in round socket like structure, comparatively less tapered, and terminal end is slightly curved to right side. Chandel et al. (1995) reported body length of fully fed first instar grubs of B. coriacea ranging from 11.0-12.7mm with an average head capsule width of 1.5+0.03mm, and these values are in the same range as recorded in the present study. The first instar grubs occupied 12.0-14.0 days under laboratory conditions at Palampur. Chandla et|al. (1988) reported somewhat higher duration of the first instar grubs of B. coriacea in Shimla hills. However, Chandel et al. (1995) reported first instar ranging from 11-20 days with an average of 14.4+0.6 days at Nauni located in mid hill region of Himachal Pradesh. To complete post eclosion development, the grubs of B. coriacea moulted twice having three distinct instars. The body length of second instar grubs was observed to range from 14.0-16.20 mm for fully fed second instar grubs. The width of head capsule varied from 2.30-2.44 mm. The second and third instar grubs did not show much difference in morphological characters, except that there was considerable increase in larval size in third instar. During this final instar, the grubs turned slightly yellowish to creamish white in colour, but the colour of the head capsule remained dark brown. The third instar grubs (Fig. 1) measured from 22.87- 26.12 mm in body length, and 3.87-4.19mm in head capsule width. The head capsule width as reported by Chandel et al., (1995) is 2.4 and 4.3 for instars II and III, and the grubs in successive instars were on an average 14.95 and 24.13mm long, respectively. Contrary to our findings, Chandla et al., (1988) reported third instar grubs of B. coriacea ranging from 35-38mm in body length. Duration of the second instar was recorded to be 23-27 days (mean + SE: 24.50+0.89 days). The third instar was recorded to be the overwintering stage, thus required 184-214 days for maturity. Although the duration of the third instar was about six months (October 2021-March 2022), but the duration of active feeding of third instar grubs was for about 60-70 days through October-November The fully fed third instar grubs stopped feeding by November, and formed hard earthen cells for overwintering and subsequent development as pupa and adults. Contrary

to this, average duration of third instar at Shimla was found to be 74.4+2.80 days (Chandla et al., 1988). Possibly they have recorded observations only on active third instar grubs before formation o earthen cells. These observations are in line with those of Veeresh (1977) for H. serrata. The total larval period was found to be 219-255 days with an average of 244+10.12 days. Gupta (2012) reported total larval period of 214-267 days under laboratory conditions at Palampur. The prepupal period in laboratory was short, ranging from 11-12 days. The pupation in laboratory was observed as early as fourth week of March 2022 in hard earthen cells. The pupa (Fig. 1) measured on an average 16.61+0.38 mm long and 7.07+0.31 mm wide. The average pupal period was 15.95+0.82 days, the minimum and maximum being 14 and 18 days, respectively. The pupal duration as reported by earlier workers was 12-20 days at
Nauni and 19-24 days at Palampur (Chandel et al., 1995; Gupta 2012). Adult formation in earthen cells was noticed in second week of April 2016 under laboratory conditions at Palampur.

The adult beetles are black in colour (Fig. 1). The body length of beetles ranged from 12.45-16.60 mm with a mean of 14.6 0.40 mm, while the width ranged from 7.47-7.98 mm with a mean of 7.54+0.35 mm as shown in table 2. The body length width of B. coriacea beetles as reported by earlier workers was in the range of 12-17.55 5.94-7.85 mm (Gupta 2012; Chandel et al,. 1995). Thus, our findings are in complete agreement with their results.
Brahmina flavosericea Brenske: In the present study, maximum abundance of B. flavosericea was recorded in Karsog area at an altitude of 1492 amsl. Pathania (2014)

has recorded this species in low to moderate numbers from Shillaroo, Bharmour, Nauni, Sangla and Kalpa areas. Chandel et al. (2010) reported that it is one of the leading species in Janjheli area of Mandi district. The young grubs were pearly white with a dark head. The fully fed first instar grubs were about 5.13+0.04 mm long (range: 4.94 to 5.32 mm). The average head capsule width was recorded to be 1.16+0.02mm. The period of first instar larvae averaged 20.0+2.13 days with extremes of 16 and 24 days. The average body length of second instar was noted to be 20.0+0.83 mm (range: 19.20-22.10 mm). The second instar had extremes of 29 and 35 days with an average of 30.2+0.12 days. The duration of third instar was found to be varying between 185 to 215 days, with an average of 203.50+5.82 days. The length of fully grown third instar larvae ranged from 23.10 to 29.0 mm (mean: 27.38+0.21mm). Mehta et al. (2008) reported larval length of 22-24 mm in fully fed grubs of B. flavosericea. The average head capsule width was recorded to be 2.14+0.02 and 3.29+0.05mm for second and third instar grubs, respectively. Pathania (2014) found head capsule width for first, second and third instar grubs of B. flavosericeaas 1.16+0.01, 2.13+0.03 and 3.30+0.04mm, respectively. These reports are well within the range observed from this species in the present study. The raster pattern is shown in fig.2. The septula is elongate, and palidia are arranged in two parallel rows. The number

of pali varied from 12 to 14, which are arranged in two parallel rows. The base of pali is spherical and the tip is pointed. The hemate setae had a circular flat base, more or less of same thickness from base to terminal end. The tip of hemate setae looks globular and is curved to right side.

The fully fed larva created a small earthen cell in the upper layer of soil in the month of November, and remained in the cell till adult emergence in next summer (Fig.2). Pupation occurred in earthen cells, and was observed as early as 17th April, 2022 in laboratory. The pupa measured from 15.74 to 17.46 mm with an average of 16.94+0.52 mm. When freshly formed they were creamy white in colour, and darkened before emergence. There was a single generation in a year. Adult formation commenced on 3rd May, 2022, and newly transformed adults remained in earthen cells for a period of about 20 days. The adults are medium sized elongate beetles. The elytra are yellowish in colour. The head and legs are dark brown in colour (Fig. 2). The length of beetles ranged from 14.44 to 16.65 mm with a mean of 15.99+ 0.21 mm, while the width ranged from 6.79 to 7.18 mm, with a mean of 7.05+0.04 mm as shown in table 3. Mehta et al. (2008) recorded length as 13-14 mm, and breadth of 7-8 mm for beetles of B. flavosericea.
Third instar larvae
Brahmina crinicollis Burmeister: The grubs of this species were collected in the month

Anshul Thakur, RS Chandel, Karthik Ramappa and Niraj Guleria
Table 3. Duration and morphometrics of different development stages of B. flavosericea
