33: <header>RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>
The survey on potato white grubs was carried out at 19 different locations in Himachal Pradesh, and 13 species of white grubs were collected as shown in table 1.

In Chamba district, Brahmina crinicollis was found to be the predominant species comprising 100 per cent population at Salooni and 68.18 per cent at Mani. At Shillaroo, Chidgaon, Matiana, Kufri and Fagu in Shimla district, only Brahmina coriacea grubs were

found to cause damage to potato tubers. Maximum population of white grubs was recorded at Shillaroo with a mean of 11.45+1.05 grubs/ft2. At Rohru and Tikkar, B. coriacea comprised 77.38 and 56.20 per cent of total grub count, respectively.
The observations recorded on morphometrics and development of different species is discussed here under.
Brahmina coriacea (Hope): The grubs caused heavy damage to potato ranging from 8.20 to 45.90 per cent in high hills of Shimla, Mandi

and Kullu districts of Himachal Pradesh. In Shimla district, B. coriacea grubs comprised 56.06 to 100.00 per cent at Tikkar, Rohru, Chidgaon, Shillaroo, Matiana, Kufri and Fagu. Chandel et al. (2003) also found B. coriacea to be a common species in Shimla hills of Himachal Pradesh. constituting about 90 per cent of the population. In Thanedhar, Kotgarh, Matiana and Jubbal areas, 100.00 per cent beetles of B. coriacea have been taken in different collections by various workers (Singh and Dogra 1984). In the present study, observations were recorded on grubs and pupae of B. coriacea under laboratory conditions at Palampur. The first instar grubs of B. coriacea were collected during July 2021 from potato fields located in Shillaroo, Matiana, Fagu and Kufri areas. The collected grubs were greyish white in colour, and their body length ranged from 6.2 to 7.3 mm. Mean head capsule width was recorded to be 1.64+0.09 mm (range: 1.59-1.74 mm). The identity of grubs was confirmed based on their raster pattern as described by Pathania et al., (2017). The raster pattern is given in fig. 1, and it is ornamented with two rows of palidia. There are 13 pali in each row. The pali of one side are crossing the corresponding pali from other side in the middle of septula. There is gradual narrowing of septula from posterior to interior direction. The hemate setae are bold and short. The palidium was broad at the base, tapered gradually, with pointed tip. The hemate setae seems to be fitted in round socket like structure, comparatively less tapered, and terminal end is slightly curved to right side. Chandel et al. (1995) reported body length of fully fed first instar grubs of B. coriacea ranging from 11.0-12.7mm with an average head capsule width of 1.5+0.03mm, and these values are in the same range as recorded in the present study. The first instar grubs occupied 12.0-14.0 days under laboratory conditions at Palampur. Chandla et|al. (1988) reported somewhat higher duration of the first instar grubs of B. coriacea in Shimla hills. However, Chandel et al. (1995) reported first instar ranging from 11-20 days with an average of 14.4+0.6 days at Nauni located in mid hill region of Himachal Pradesh. To complete post eclosion development, the grubs of B. coriacea moulted twice having three distinct instars. The body length of second instar grubs was observed to range from 14.0-16.20 mm for fully fed second instar grubs. The width of head capsule varied from 2.30-2.44 mm. The second and third instar grubs did not show much difference in morphological characters, except that there was considerable increase in larval size in third instar. During this final instar, the grubs turned slightly yellowish to creamish white in colour, but the colour of the head capsule remained dark brown. The third instar grubs (Fig. 1) measured from 22.87- 26.12 mm in body length, and 3.87-4.19mm in head capsule width. The head capsule width as reported by Chandel et al., (1995) is 2.4 and 4.3 for instars II and III, and the grubs in successive instars were on an average 14.95 and 24.13mm long, respectively. Contrary to our findings, Chandla et al., (1988) reported third instar grubs of B. coriacea ranging from 35-38mm in body length. Duration of the second instar was recorded to be 23-27 days (mean + SE: 24.50+0.89 days). The third instar was recorded to be the overwintering stage, thus required 184-214 days for maturity. Although the duration of the third instar was about six months (October 2021-March 2022), but the duration of active feeding of third instar grubs was for about 60-70 days through October-November The fully fed third instar grubs stopped feeding by November, and formed hard earthen cells for overwintering and subsequent development as pupa and adults. Contrary

to this, average duration of third instar at Shimla was found to be 74.4+2.80 days (Chandla et al., 1988). Possibly they have recorded observations only on active third instar grubs before formation o earthen cells. These observations are in line with those of Veeresh (1977) for H. serrata. The total larval period was found to be 219-255 days with an average of 244+10.12 days. Gupta (2012) reported total larval period of 214-267 days under laboratory conditions at Palampur. The prepupal period in laboratory was short, ranging from 11-12 days. The pupation in laboratory was observed as early as fourth week of March 2022 in hard earthen cells. The pupa (Fig. 1) measured on an average 16.61+0.38 mm long and 7.07+0.31 mm wide. The average pupal period was 15.95+0.82 days, the minimum and maximum being 14 and 18 days, respectively. The pupal duration as reported by earlier workers was 12-20 days at
Nauni and 19-24 days at Palampur (Chandel et al., 1995; Gupta 2012). Adult formation in earthen cells was noticed in second week of April 2016 under laboratory conditions at Palampur.

The adult beetles are black in colour (Fig. 1). The body length of beetles ranged from 12.45-16.60 mm with a mean of 14.6 0.40 mm, while the width ranged from 7.47-7.98 mm with a mean of 7.54+0.35 mm as shown in table 2. The body length width of B. coriacea beetles as reported by earlier workers was in the range of 12-17.55 5.94-7.85 mm (Gupta 2012; Chandel et al,. 1995). Thus, our findings are in complete agreement with their results.
Brahmina flavosericea Brenske: In the present study, maximum abundance of B. flavosericea was recorded in Karsog area at an altitude of 1492 amsl. Pathania (2014)

has recorded this species in low to moderate numbers from Shillaroo, Bharmour, Nauni, Sangla and Kalpa areas. Chandel et al. (2010) reported that it is one of the leading species in Janjheli area of Mandi district. The young grubs were pearly white with a dark head. The fully fed first instar grubs were about 5.13+0.04 mm long (range: 4.94 to 5.32 mm). The average head capsule width was recorded to be 1.16+0.02mm. The period of first instar larvae averaged 20.0+2.13 days with extremes of 16 and 24 days. The average body length of second instar was noted to be 20.0+0.83 mm (range: 19.20-22.10 mm). The second instar had extremes of 29 and 35 days with an average of 30.2+0.12 days. The duration of third instar was found to be varying between 185 to 215 days, with an average of 203.50+5.82 days. The length of fully grown third instar larvae ranged from 23.10 to 29.0 mm (mean: 27.38+0.21mm). Mehta et al. (2008) reported larval length of 22-24 mm in fully fed grubs of B. flavosericea. The average head capsule width was recorded to be 2.14+0.02 and 3.29+0.05mm for second and third instar grubs, respectively. Pathania (2014) found head capsule width for first, second and third instar grubs of B. flavosericeaas 1.16+0.01, 2.13+0.03 and 3.30+0.04mm, respectively. These reports are well within the range observed from this species in the present study. The raster pattern is shown in fig.2. The septula is elongate, and palidia are arranged in two parallel rows. The number

of pali varied from 12 to 14, which are arranged in two parallel rows. The base of pali is spherical and the tip is pointed. The hemate setae had a circular flat base, more or less of same thickness from base to terminal end. The tip of hemate setae looks globular and is curved to right side.

The fully fed larva created a small earthen cell in the upper layer of soil in the month of November, and remained in the cell till adult emergence in next summer (Fig.2). Pupation occurred in earthen cells, and was observed as early as 17th April, 2022 in laboratory. The pupa measured from 15.74 to 17.46 mm with an average of 16.94+0.52 mm. When freshly formed they were creamy white in colour, and darkened before emergence. There was a single generation in a year. Adult formation commenced on 3rd May, 2022, and newly transformed adults remained in earthen cells for a period of about 20 days. The adults are medium sized elongate beetles. The elytra are yellowish in colour. The head and legs are dark brown in colour (Fig. 2). The length of beetles ranged from 14.44 to 16.65 mm with a mean of 15.99+ 0.21 mm, while the width ranged from 6.79 to 7.18 mm, with a mean of 7.05+0.04 mm as shown in table 3. Mehta et al. (2008) recorded length as 13-14 mm, and breadth of 7-8 mm for beetles of B. flavosericea.
Third instar larvae
Brahmina crinicollis Burmeister: The grubs of this species were collected in the month

Anshul Thakur, RS Chandel, Karthik Ramappa and Niraj Guleria

of July at Salooni, Mani, Bagsiad and Janjheli in the present study. The head capsule width for third instar grubs ranged from 2.99 to 3.47 mm with an average of 3.25+0.06mm. The fully fed third instar grubs measured on an average 26.24+1.03mm in length (range: 23.51 to 29.8mm). The grubs continued feeding on potato tubers throughout August to October. The raster pattern (fig. 3) can be identified by two rows of palidia, each consisting of 18-20 pali. Each palidium runs parallel to each other from anterior to posterior direction upto 12 pali, but after 12 pali, each palidium diverge in opposite direction in a typical semi-circle forming J-shaped pattern as shown in fig.3. During November, fully fed third instar grubs constructed earthen cells and overwintering occurred in quiescent larval stage in these earthen cells from November to March. In the first week of April, grubs transformed into pupae inside the earthen cells. The pupation in laboratory

was observed as early as 4th April, 2022. The pupa measured on an average 16.53+0.41mm long, and 7.08+0.42mm wide. The average pupal period was 17.95+0.74 days under laboratory condition, the minimum and maximum being 16 and 20 days, respectively. Total duration of third instar was calculated to 190 to 220 days (mean: 205.60+5.7 days), and there was one generation per year.

Adult formation inside earthen cells was noticed as early as 18th April. Adult beetles are shiny black in colour, but the head and thorax are covered distinctly with brown hairs showing clear cut distinction from the rest of the body. The beetles measured 14.68 to 16.98mm in length, and 6.76 to 7.46mm (range: 6.76-7.46mm) in width.
Holotrichia longipennis Blanchard: The grubs of H. longipennis were collected at Shimla in potato fields. According to Thakur (2000), this species usually occurs in northern India
Third instar larvae
Raster pattern
Pupae
Beetle
Fig. 3 Development stages and raster pattern of B. crinicollis

and Pakistan at altitude varying from 700- 2500 amsl. The average density in potato fields was recorded to be 4.15+0.17 grubs/ ft2 (range: 2 to 6 grubs/ft2. The collected grubs were in first instar, and were further reared in laboratory till adult emergence. The field collected first instar grubs were creamy white in colour with light brown head which was prominently broader from rest of the body. The head capsule width of first instar averaged 1.73+0.03 mm. Mean body length of fully fed first instar was recorded to be 8.88+0.14 mm (range: 8.28 to 9.14 mm). Shah and Shah (1990) reported body length of early first instar grubs of H. longipennis as 6.92+1.64 mm, and 1.97+0.28 mm as width from first thoracic segment. Pathania et al. (2016) found body length of first instar larvae of H. longipennis to vary from 8.35 to 9.1 mm; and the average head capsule width has been reported to be 1.74+0.01 mm. These reports are well within the range observed for this species in the present study. Under laboratory condition, the duration of first instar was recorded to be 14 to 18 days. Pathania et al. (2016) reported first instar of H. longipennis ranging from 21 to 26 days in Himachal Pradesh. However, in Uttarakhand, earlier workers have indicated higher duration ranging from 40.08-41.8 days for first instar grubs of H. longipennis.

The first instar grubs moulted into second instar by the end of August. The body colour of grubs in second instar was dirty white with dark brown head capsule. The body length ranged from 18.9 to 20.14 mm for fully fed second instar grubs. The head capsule width varied from 3.32 to 3.67 mm. Mishra et al. (1998) reported body length of second instar of H. longipennis ranging from 23.5 to 25 mm in Uttarakhand. The head capsule width reported by Pathania et al. (2016) is 3.15+0.04 mm. These reports clearly substantiate our results. The duration

of second instar lasted for 26 to 32 days with an average of 29.20+0.81 days. The second instar grubs of H. longipennis occurred from August to October. The second instar grubs constructed earthen cells and moulted inside these cells to the third instar. According to Kalpana (2015), the average duration for second instar of H. longipennis was 35.06+0.52 days for this species at Palampur. During the final instar, the grubs turned greyish white in colour, measured from 32.88 to 34.31 mm in body length, and 5.03 to 5.13 mm in head capsule width. Shah and Shah (1990) reported that third instar of H. longipennis are 31.69 mm long indicating almost similar size of final instar grubs, whereas head capsule width reported by them is on higher side (8.91 mm). The raster pattern is given in fig. 4. Raster is equipped with two widely separated prominent palidia. The palidia (comb like structure) and hemate setae (lateral to palidia on each side -Tegillum) covered nearly half of the last ventral abdominal segment. Each palidium possessed long, sharp 13-14 pali. The septula (space between two palidia) iswide, linear andappears to be narrow 'V'shaped. Tegilla occupied nearly caudal half of the area between loweranal lip and theanterior margin of the last abdominal segment. The description of raster pattern matches with that of Kalpana (2015) and Pathania et al. (2016).
The duration of third instar ranges from 207-228 days with an average of 218+3.12
Third instar larvae Raster pattern Beetle Fig. 4 Development stages and raster pattern of H. longipennis

days. The third instar grubs occurred from September to April. The average period of third instar of H. longipennis at Ranichauri was found to be 211.6+3.14 days (Mishra et al. 1998). Haq (1962) reported that the duration of third instar grubs of H. longipennis in Uttarakhand ranges from 160-191 days with a mean value of 175.4 days. The fully fed third instar grubs moved downward, stopped feeding and constructed earthen cells. The formation of earthen cells by third instar grubs was noticed in the month of November. Third instar grubs produced large irregular cavities deep into the potato tubers, and such tubers are rendered totally unfit for marketing. Total larval period ranged from 247-303 days. The pupation in laboratory was observed as early as 14 April, 2022. The average pupal period was recorded to be 14.0+0.86 days under laboratory condition. The minimum and maximum being 12 and 16 days. According to Shah and Shah (1990), the pupal period was of 22 to 28 days at Nainital for this species. It was of 15 to 23 days at Ranichauri (Mishra et al. 1998). In laboratory, the adult formation was noticed in the first week of May. Pathania et al. (2016) also noticed adult formation of H. longipennis inside earthen cells as early as 7th May at Palampur. The adult beetles of H. longipennis are shining light chestnut brown in colour with light brown abdomen and dark brown legs. The average body length X width of beetle was recorded to be 21.61+0.34 X 9.08+0.05 mm. Kalpana (2015) recorded length of 21.29 to 21.49 mm and breadth of 8.94 to 9.27 mm for beetles of H. longipennis in Himachal Pradesh. There was one generation in year.

Anomala rufiventris Redtenbacher: The grubs of A. rufiventris were collected at Janjheli and Nihri in Mandi district, and Rohru in Shimla district of Himachal Pradesh. A. rufiventris has been recorded on light trap in Sirmour district by Chandra (2005). Kumar et al. (2007)

collected A. rufiventris on light trap in Kullu valley. In Kumaon hills of Uttrakhand, A. rufiventris comprised 0.18-0.31 per cent of total beetle catch on light trap (Sushil et al. 2006). The grubs were further reared in laboratory at Palampur. The fully fed third instar grubs averaged 42.06+1.93mm in their body length with a range of 40.13 to 43.98mm. The head capsule width of third instar grubs averaged 4.29+0.12mm (range: 4.17 to 4.4mm). The raster pattern is given in fig.5. The raster is characterised by very long and thin pali. The pali of each side overlap corresponding pali of other side in the centre, and pali are arranged in a clustered manner. The grubs did not inflict much damage, but superficial scrapping was seen on potato tubers. The fully-fed larvae made a small earthen cell in deep layer of soil (about 6 to 8 cm deep) on first November, and remained in the cell till adult emergence in next summer. The grubs remained active for about 90 days before constructing earthen cells. They spent about 180 days in earthen cells before transforming into pupae. Pupa was observed as early as 2 May, 2002 in laboratory. The length of pupal stage was found to vary from 24 to 27 days with an average of 25.33+0.88days. The adult formation commenced on 26 May, 2022. There was single generation per year, and the total average duration of the third instar and pupa was observed to be 288+2.13days. The adults are medium sized, elongate beetles. Their body is smooth, shining, very dark bronzy- green, sternum and legs greenish black and
Third instar larvae
Raster pattern
Beetle
Fig. 5 Development stages and raster pattern of A. rufiventris

ventral abdominal segments mahogany red. Pronotum thinly and very minutely punctate. The length of beetles ranged from 16.10 to 22.00 mm, while the width varied from 10 to 11mm. Rai and Joshi (1988) has reported A. rufiventris causing damage to potato in Uttarakhand. According to Chandra and Uniyal (2007), A. rufiventrisis distributed in Himachal Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal, Manipur, Meghalaya, Sikkim and Bhutan.

Melolontha cuprescens Blanchard: The grubs of M. cuprescens were collected from potato fields in Khadrala area of Shimla district. It is said by Chandra (2005) to occur from Chamba, Kinnaur and Kullu district of Himachal Pradesh. Pathania (2014) has reported M. cuprescens in Holi, Kheradhar, Dalash and Sangla. The average population of grubs in soil was recorded to be 7.82+0.50 grubs/ft2. The grubs were large in size, and measured 58.74+1.28 mm (range: 51.98- 68.02mm) in body length. The average head capsule width was recorded to be 6.5+0.15 mm (range: 51.98-68.02 mm). The grubs were collected in the month of November and were maintained in laboratory for about two months. The grubs inflicted large holes in potato tubers during feeding. The grubs stopped feeding by the end of November and hibernated in large earthen cells. There was no pupa formation in the laboratory. The raster pattern is given in fig. 6. The raster is prominently conspicous with two
Third instar larvae Raster pattern Fig. 6 Development stages and raster pattern of M. cuprescens

widely separated and prominent palidia in central region. The paired palidia are nearly parallel each consisting of 28-29 pali, pointed downward especially in the lower region. The anal slit is Y shaped, depressed and hooked in the centre, slightly raised on both sides and gradually tapering laterally in a semi-circular fashion. The anal cleft is straight, touching the base of septula. Rana et al. (2022) also revealed similar features in his description of raster pattern of M. cuprescens.

Melolontha furcicauda Ancey: The grubs of M. furcicauda were collected from potato fields at Rohru during November. The grubs were collected in very few numbers in potato fields. The head capsule width was recorded to be 6.89 mm, and the body length averaged 59.03 mm. M. furcicaudahas been reported to be an important pest infesting oats during seedling stage in northern Kashmir (Kumar et al., 2019). Gupta et al. (1977) has reported M. furcicaudaas a major pest of different fruit crops including apple, apricot and cherry in different regions of north-western Himalaya. The raster pattern is given in fig. 7. The raster pattern of M. furcicauda has been described by Rana et al. (2022), and it matches with our picture. Each palidium consisted of 26 pali. The anal slit is raised in the centre and gradually tapers to both sides uniformly.
Polyphylla sikkimensis Brenske: The grubs of P. sikkimensis were collected from Barot and Janjheli in Mandi district inflicting
Third instar larvae Raster pattern Fig. 7 Development stages and raster pattern of M. furcicauda

considerable damage to potato tubers. The head capsule width measured 7.6+0.17 mm (range: 7.32 to 7.98 mm), and body length was recorded to be varying from 58.3 to 63.12 mm (59.8+1.2 mm). The grubs were collected from Janjheli in the month of July, 2021, and were reared in laboratory on potato tubers upto November. The grubs constructed earthen cells in November for hibernation. Pupa formation was not recorded under laboratory condition. The raster pattern is given in fig. 8. The palidia is short and each palidium consisted of 12 pali. The pali pointed inward and upward in the upper region with septula indistinct. Anal cleft is absent. The V shape anal slit, more or less flat in the centre, equal gradually on both side and becomes almost flat for considered end on both sides. Rana (2020) has described the raster pattern of P. sikkimensis in a similar manner. In Polyphylla disoniata, the anal opening is broadly V shape and rasterly too short, nearly parallel, longitudinal palidia each sparsely set with 7-12 cylindrical pali whose tips frequently touching those of opposite palidium (Ritcher 1966).

Unidentified Species I-V: The grubs of unidentified species I were collected at Mani, and Benchi areas. The head capsule width of grubs averaged 4.92+0.14 mm (range: 4.87- 5.12 mm), and body length was 33.78 mm (range: 31.98-39.53 mm). The raster pattern is given in fig. 9. The unidentified species II was collected at Rohru in the beginning of November. The head capsule width of grubs averaged 3.72+0.19 mm (range: 3.50 to 4.11mm), and mean body length measured 41.2+1.2 mm (range: 39.2 to 45.10 mm). The raster pattern is given in fig. 10.

The grubs of unidentified species III were collected at Tikkar (2244 m amsl) in Shimla district and Anni (2006 m amsl) in Kullu district. The head capsule width was recorded to be 3.45+0.13 mm (range: 3.29 to 3.67 mm)

The unidentified species IV was collected at Benchi. The head capsule width of grubs was recorded to be 3.81 mm, and body length averaged 32.36 mm. The raster pattern is given in fig. 12.

The grubs of unidentified species V were collected at Nihri in Mandi district, and Banjaar and Anni in Kullu district. The fully fed third instar grubs averaged 28.98+0.52 mm in their body length with a range of 27.36-31.23mm. The head capsule width of

The adults are medium sized elongate beetles. The elytra are shining brown in colour. The head and legs are dark brown in colour (Fig.13). The length of beetles ranged from 12.49-16.7 m with a mean of 15.05+0.94 mm, while the width ranged from 7.13-7.89 mm with a mean of 7.46+0.16 mm.
34: <header>CONCLUSION </header>

Lot of diversity exists in white grubs causing damage to potato which is grown under rainfed condition in high hills of Himachal Pradesh, under long day conditions. In total, 13 species were recorded from potato fields, and frequency of their occurrence is very high. B. coriacea is a leading species in Shimla and Mandi, whereas in Chamba area, B. crinicollis constituted greater percentage. Similarly in Mandi district, P. sikkimensis also inflicted considerable damage and warrants control measures. The existing recommendation of white grubs is based on study conducted in Shimla hills where B. coriacea is the only species. Therefore, there is need to develop management practices for the control of other species like B. crinicollis and P. sikkimensis, in their respective areas of occurrence. There is also need to popularize the available technology with more emphasis on biointensive management as none of sample potato growers were found to use any biopesticide to control white grubs.

0: <title>RECENT CHALLENGES AND FUTURE SUSTAINABILITY OF POTATO CULTIVATION IN HUGLI DISTRICT, WEST BENGAL: A GEOGRAPHICAL APPRAISAL USING SWOT-AHP ANALYSIS </title>
Benojir Yasmin¹* Arindam Roy1, Mehedi Hasan Mandal2 and Giyasuddin Siddique¹

ABSTRACT: The prime goal of the present study is to ascertain suitable strategies regarding the sustainable and effective growth of potato cultivation in Hugli district of West Bengal by examining the Strengths (S), Weaknesses (W), Opportunities (O) and Threats (T) of the system through a combination of SWOT analysis and Analytical Hierarchy Process. The result reveals that constant year round demand of potato (S5, 0.25) and increasing expenditure costs (W3, 0.25) are the most prioritized internal strength and weakness respectively. On the contrary, large scale employment generation (O6, 0.29) for the locals as well as the migrant laborers is the most prioritized external opportunity, whereas, oscillating nature of economic profitability (T2, 0.27) is the most prioritized external threat of potato cultivation in the area. The overall priority scores of SWOT analysis exhibits that steady demand of potato throughout the year (S5) possesses the most significant (9.7%) aspect among all the internal and external factors. In conclusion, appropriate strategies have been formulated by linking the most prioritized 'strength-opportunity' and 'weakness-threat' factors for future possibilities of the potato cultivation in Hugli district of West Bengal.
KEYWORDS: Potato, SWOT Analysis, Analytic Hierarchy Process, Hugli

Benojir Yasmin¹* Arindam Roy1, Mehedi Hasan Mandal2 and Giyasuddin Siddique¹

ABSTRACT: The prime goal of the present study is to ascertain suitable strategies regarding the sustainable and effective growth of potato cultivation in Hugli district of West Bengal by examining the Strengths (S), Weaknesses (W), Opportunities (O) and Threats (T) of the system through a combination of SWOT analysis and Analytical Hierarchy Process. The result reveals that constant year round demand of potato (S5, 0.25) and increasing expenditure costs (W3, 0.25) are the most prioritized internal strength and weakness respectively. On the contrary, large scale employment generation (O6, 0.29) for the locals as well as the migrant laborers is the most prioritized external opportunity, whereas, oscillating nature of economic profitability (T2, 0.27) is the most prioritized external threat of potato cultivation in the area. The overall priority scores of SWOT analysis exhibits that steady demand of potato throughout the year (S5) possesses the most significant (9.7%) aspect among all the internal and external factors. In conclusion, appropriate strategies have been formulated by linking the most prioritized 'strength-opportunity' and 'weakness-threat' factors for future possibilities of the potato cultivation in Hugli district of West Bengal.
KEYWORDS: Potato, SWOT Analysis, Analytic Hierarchy Process, Hugli
2: <header>INTRODUCTION </header>

Endowed with suitable agro-ecological conditions, the Hugli district has acclaimed a remarkable position in potato (Solanum tuberosum) cultivation since the 1970s with massive production and superior productivity in comparison to the other districts of West Bengal (Mookherjee et al., 2012). Being introduced as a food crop, the potato has briskly emerged as the prime cash crop in the area on account of the substantial demand, which has persuaded the farmers to shift to potato cultivation (Ghosh, 2017). Since then potato has become the principal choice of the farmers due to higher profitability. Within a very short period of time the crop has proved its importance in the rural economy. More

than 1/3rd of the geographical area and almost 3/4th farmers of the district are engaged in potato cultivation (Ghosh, 2017). Despite significant spatio-temporal dynamicity in the area, production and yield, potato has retained its superiority in the larger part of the district, even ahead of paddy due to its capability to compensate the loss incurred by other crops (Roy et al., 2019). At present, the district holds a leading position in production and export of potato to the nearby districts as well as states. Such constant expansion of market and increment in demand lead towards the escalation of production volume and greater involvement of arable land, labour and capital. Recent developments of infrastructural facilities like quality roads and cold storages, encouraging governmental
policies have added extra impetus for success of potato cultivation in the district (Ghosh, 2017).

Despite the present flourishing state with a wider scope of future prosperity, potato cultivation in the district has experienced diverse physical, economic and policy oriented problems affecting the production, marketing and pricing of potato. The economic state of the potato growers, especially the small and marginal farmers, bargadars and share croppers, often encountered with uncertainty and disheartened them to grow potato. Such adverse circumstances have hindered the overall consistency of potato farming in recent days. Therefore, it is necessary to formulate suitable strategies for future sustainability through proper understanding of the functionality of potato farming in the area. In such context, the present study strives to examine the potency and flaws of the existing farming system in the area, explore the future opportunities as well as ascertain the upcoming threats and finally determine
the suitable strategy for future sustainability of potato cultivation in the district using SWOT-AHP analysis.

5: <header>MATERIALS AND METHODS </header>
The study area

The Hugli district is located in the south- central region of the state of West Bengal along the western bank of River Hooghly. The administrative boundaries extend between 23°01'20"N - 22°39'32" N and 88°30'15"E - 87°39'32"E. The district is bounded by the river Hugli in the east, Paschim Medinipur district in the west, Bankura and Purba Barddhaman districts in the north, and Howrah to the south. Located on the flat alluvial plains of lower Gangetic region, the district is served by rivers like Hugli, Damodar, Dwarakeswar, Saraswati etc. and endowed with rich, easily extractable ground water resources (Roy et al., 2019). The district comes under one of the top potato producing districts in West Bengal. Therefore the district has been deliberately selected for the present

study. The district lies in the tropical humid monsoon climatic regime with oppressive hot summer, high humidity nearly all the year round and well distributed rainfall during the monsoon season. The district is characterized with three distinct seasons: hot summer, wet monsoon and cold dry winter. May is the hottest month, with the mean daily maximum temperature of 36.7°C and the mean daily minimum temperature at 24.3°C. On the contrary, January is the coldest month with the mean daily maximum temperature of about 26.6°C and the mean daily minimum temperature of about 12.1°C. The cold season starts from the middle of November and continues up to end of February. The average annual rainfall in the district is 1418.5 mm. The maximum rainfall occurs during the southwest monsoon season. Nearly 76% of the annual rainfall occurs during June to September. The relative humidity reaches above 90% in August and recedes to as low as 78% in April-May (IMD, 2008).

8: <header>Sources and nature of data </header>

The present study is based on the primary data, obtained through intensive field investigation and direct interaction with the stakeholders. Both personal interaction and focus group discussions (FGDs) were conducted to appraise individual to community perception concerning the pros and cons of the present management of potato from cultivation to marketing. For this purpose, 60 farmers, 72 bargadars (including share croppers and contract farmers), 80 casual labourers (engaged in harvesting, grading, sorting, packaging, preserving, transportation etc.), 40 wholesalers (or agents/ intermediaries/ middlemen), 40 retailers, 30 women workers (engaged in industries of potato chips, potato powder making) and 5 cold storage owners are interviewed with semi-structured questionnaire, containing both open ended and close ended questions. All the respondents

were selected through random sampling techniques and the interview session was conducted during the harvesting seasons of 2019-2020. Among the 60 sampled cultivators, eight farmers are large farmers (having > 4 hectares of land), 12 are medium (2-4 hectare), 14 small (1-2 hectare) and 26 marginal (< 1 hectare) farmers. Furthermore, four focus group discussions (FGDs), involving 12 stakeholders in each group (engaged in potato farming for at least 40 years) have been conducted to acquire information regarding the evolution and changing aspects of potato production system in the district.

10: <header>1. SWOT-AHP Analysis </header>

Introduced by Albert Humphrey in 1960s, the SWOT model comprises an effective decision making model by analyzing two prevailing or internal factors i.e. strengths (S) and weaknesses (W), and two upcoming or external factors i.e., opportunities (O) and threats (T) of any system (Gürel and Tat, 2017). This effective strategic tool has been used in the present study accompaying with the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) to understand the determinants and their inter- connections and to formulate the strategies for future sustainability of potato cultivation. The influencing internal and external factors of potato cultivation in the study area has been highlighted in Table 1.

The Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) is one of the most efficient multi-criteria decision making technique (MCDM) which has widely been used to solve many real problems (Oreski, 2012). In this mathematical model, the alternatives in each category of SWOT matrix are ranked according to a certain weight based on their relative importance (Görener et al., 2012). The criteria weights of each SWOT elements are assigned by using Saaty's fundamental scale (1980) which denotes the relative intensity of

Opportunities (O)|Threats (T) O1 Extension of agro based and food processing industries|T1 Occurrences of crop failures O2 Cultivation of different varieties of potato|T2 Fluctuation in economic profitability 03 Expansion of agricultural markets and escalation in demand|T3 Over production and declining market prices O4 Implementation of suitable policies regarding storing (like Kishan Mandi), marketing and trading|T4 Shifting of farmers from potato to other crops O5 Improvement in infrastructural facilities|T5 Depletion of ground water level O6 Large scale employment opportunities for the locals and the migrant labourers|T6 Deterioration of soil quality owing to excessive use of chemical fertilizers, pesticides etc and risk of biomagnifications O7 Establishment and efficient management of large scale farms and participatory management system|T7 Recent changes in climate
importance between two alternative factors (Sharma et al. 2008).

The pair-wise comparison matrix, resembling to square and reciprocal matrix of each factor within each group, is computed by using the following equation to measure the relative priorities and find out the most influential elements (Alonso and Lamata, 2006). In this matrix, every element is i (i,j = 1,2,...,n) the quotient weight of the criteria where the value of wi vary from 1 to 9.
In the next step, each matrix is normalized to compute the relative weights by multiplying
the actual eigen vector (w) with corresponding largest eigen value (Amax), following the equation below (Lee and Walsh, 2011):

The Consistency Ratio (CR) helps measure the consistency of the result of pair- wise comparison in AHP where 0.1 refers to the accepted upper limit of CR. If the final Consistency Ratio exceeds this threshold value, then it indicates the inconsistency and the whole evaluation process needs to be repeated to improve the consistency
(Ergu et al., 2011). The Consistency Ratio is determined from the ratio between the Consistency Index (CI) and Random Index (RI) which is as follows (Borajee and Yakchali, 2011):
CR=Cl = RI
The overall priority of the SWOT factor have been computed by multiplying the priority of the factor within the group by the priority of the group (Kurttila et al., 2000).

The pair-wise comparison matrix, resembling to square and reciprocal matrix of each factor within each group, is computed by using the following equation to measure the relative priorities and find out the most influential elements (Alonso and Lamata, 2006). In this matrix, every element is i (i,j = 1,2,...,n) the quotient weight of the criteria where the value of wi vary from 1 to 9.
In the next step, each matrix is normalized to compute the relative weights by multiplying
the actual eigen vector (w) with corresponding largest eigen value (Amax), following the equation below (Lee and Walsh, 2011):
Aw = AmaxW
Then, the Consistency Index (CI) is calculated by using the following formula (Sharma et al., 2008):

The Consistency Ratio (CR) helps measure the consistency of the result of pair- wise comparison in AHP where 0.1 refers to the accepted upper limit of CR. If the final Consistency Ratio exceeds this threshold value, then it indicates the inconsistency and the whole evaluation process needs to be repeated to improve the consistency
(Ergu et al., 2011). The Consistency Ratio is determined from the ratio between the Consistency Index (CI) and Random Index (RI) which is as follows (Borajee and Yakchali, 2011):
CR=Cl = RI
The overall priority of the SWOT factor have been computed by multiplying the priority of the factor within the group by the priority of the group (Kurttila et al., 2000).
12: <header>1. SWOT-AHP Analysis </header>

Introduced by Albert Humphrey in 1960s, the SWOT model comprises an effective decision making model by analyzing two prevailing or internal factors i.e. strengths (S) and weaknesses (W), and two upcoming or external factors i.e., opportunities (O) and threats (T) of any system (Gürel and Tat, 2017). This effective strategic tool has been used in the present study accompaying with the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) to understand the determinants and their inter- connections and to formulate the strategies for future sustainability of potato cultivation. The influencing internal and external factors of potato cultivation in the study area has been highlighted in Table 1.

The Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) is one of the most efficient multi-criteria decision making technique (MCDM) which has widely been used to solve many real problems (Oreski, 2012). 14: <header>2. Formulation of strategic planning </header>

A multi-criterion strategic framework has been prepared for effective decision making in futuristic developments of potato production by linking the most prioritized S-O and W-T factors. The perception based empirical approach has been followed here to link the most prioritized six positive factors and six negative factors to formulate the strategies.

<list>(a) Strength-Opportunity (SO): SO strategy connects the most prioritized three internal strenghts with three external oppoutunities to enhance the prospect of potato production 
(b) Weakness-Threat (WT): WT strategy links the most prioritized three internal weaknesses with three external threats</list>
that suggests the alternative action planning to combat the major risks and shortcomings of potato production

The strategy having the highest recognition from respondents has been chosen as the best potential strategy for future sustainability and development of potato farming in the district. The views of the respondents have been collected by perception survey through a rating scale, classified into three categories i.e. agree, disagree and neutral (Chakraborty and Chakma, 2020).
15: <header>Analysis of the SWOT factors </header>

The Comparison Matrix of strength factors (Table 2) reveals that permanence in demand round the year (S5, importance degree value: 0.25) is the highest strength of potato production in the studied district followed by favorable agro-climatic conditions (S1, value: 0.23), and high cropping intensity and production rate (S2, value: 0.18). The demand of potato as cheap source of starch has every prospect to sustain long rather will increase with its use as different varieties of food items. The usage of potato in traditional Bengali diet is highly appreciated due to its taste, good nutrition value and affordable price. Naturally, there is a consistent demand
Table 2: Comparison matrix of strength group

Strengths (S)|S1|S2|S3|S4|S5|S6|S7|Importance Degrees S1 Favorable agro-climatic conditions|1.00|3.00|3.00|2.00|0.33|2.00|5.00|0.23 S2 High cropping intensity and production rate|0.33|1.00|2.00|2.00|1.00|3.00|4.00|0.18 S3 Long experience of potato cultivators (more than 40 years)|0.33|0.50|1.00|1.00|0.50|3.00|3.00|0.12 S4 Short duration crop that fits well within the prevailing cropping system|0.50|0.50|1.00|1.00|0.50|2.00|3.00|0.11 S5 Continuity in demand throughout the year|3.00|1.00|2.00|2.00|1.00|3.00|4.00|0.25 S6 Availability of local labour forces|0.50|0.33|0.33|0.50|0.33|1.00|4.00|0.08 S7 Farmer's willingness and massive engagement|0.20|0.25|0.33|0.33|0.25|0.25|1.00|0.04 C.R = 0.07||||||||

of potato in household level for consumption in daily basis which assumes that the internal demand is expected to be in an inflated state. Recent rapid booming of urbanization in the eastern margin of the district along the right bank of the river Hugli and consequent increment in number of census towns during the last decade (Siddique and Mukherjee, 2017) has enhanced the demand. Besides, the increasing commercial demand in newly flourished food-processing units in and around the district has enhanced the demand side. As the area under review is located close to the Kolkata metropolitan city and Haora, the area experiences a steady demand of potato. In addition, potatoes from this belt have been exported to the neighboring states also. Such escalated demands promises greater possibility of prosperity in production in near or distant future to meet the growing demands. Concurrently, the crop has huge potentiality to cope up with the increased food demand and poverty alleviation. The need for assured food security to the ever growing population with limited availability of cultivable land has necessitated spatio- temporal intensification of cropping. As a short duration cash crop, potato has easily accustomed with the existing cropping system and shows notable capability of adjustment with the changes in the system. Moreover, the crop has revealed its greater suitability in the varied agro-climatic conditions of the district. According to the Comprehensive District Agricultural Plan (CDAP) Report (2012) and Agricultural Technology Management Agency (ATMA) Report (2005-06) the district can be divided into three distinct agro-ecological situation zones (AES), of which AES I and II, covering nearly 83.77% of the district's area, are extensively suitable for potato cultivation. Almost 65.04% area under these two zones has already been used for potato farming. Even 21.62% area in AES III where monoculture

has been practiced, is also engaged in potato cultivation (Ghosh, 2017). In a nutshell, it may be said that the physical environment of the district is not a barrier; rather it provides an extensive field for potato farming.

The Table 3 has shown the comparison matrix of weakness aspects of the system. The analysis reveals that increasing expenditure costs and decreasing profit (W3, importance degree value: 0.25) are the most dominating weaknesses of the persisting agricultural system of the area. The cost of each component of agricultural input like chemical fertilizers, pesticides, labour, HYV seeds etc. along with transportation, irrigation, cold storage facilities has conspicuously increased over the past years. Field survey has revealed that the cost of chemical fertilizers and pesticides has been increased nearly 136.45% and 41.67% respectively from 2000 to 2019. Similarly, the cost of HYV seeds is risen about 68.75%, labour cost is increased nearly 160%, and irrigation cost is enhanced up to 200% during the stipulated phase. Overall, the estimated production cost per hectare area has been incremented to 111% during past twenty years. On an average, the irrigation cost comprises almost 9.47% of the total cost in 2019-20. As a rabi crop, potato is generally cultivated in the district during the dry months of the winter (October- February). Naturally, potato cultivation has to depend upon irrigation water. Therefore, huge amount of ground water has been extensively extracted through deep, shallow or submersible pumps for irrigation purpose, which has alarmingly reduced the level of ground water table. The rising production expenditure naturally reduces the profit of the producers. Most notable fact is that, the economic return from the field has failed to keep pace with mounting production cost over the past years. Even in a number of cases, the farmers have failed to recover their
Benojir Yasmin, Arindam Roy, Mehedi Hasan Mandal and Giyasuddin Siddique

investments. Under such circumstances, the small and marginal farmers have become most vulnerable due to their incapability to combat the economic loss and sustain their livelihoods properly. They prefer loans from local money lenders [Mahajans] with high rate of interest (ranging from 10% to 15% per month during 2019-2020) to compensate for the loss and further beginning of cultivation and ultimately get trapped in debt. Thus the profit from the field of the potato growers gradually decreases which acts as a major economic constraints for potato cultivation.

Apart from this, the irregularities in market owing to the tricks of the middlemen (W5, value: 0.167), and climatic variability (W1, value: 0.15) are found to be another two most influencing internal weakness factors. The artificial crisis of potato in the market are often created by the middlemen (forias, aratdars) and the distributors for pocketing the benefit of inflated price where the economically weak small and marginal farmers are forced to sell the product at lower prices just after harvest and the middlemen multiply their profit each year. Due to the economic insolvency, the small and marginal
farmers often sell the crop from the field at a cheap cost in order to save the storage cost as the harvested products are non- preservable, easily rotten and damaged by diverse diseases.

The labour intensive nature of farming in the area with less use of modern machineries generates a huge demand of skilled and unskilled labourers in different stages from field preparation to marketing of potato. The greater potentialities of major agro-based industrial sector may create a wide scope of employment opportunities in near future. A bulk of poor fellow from the nearby rural areas or even from the neighboring districts migrate as agricultural labourers towards the area during harvesting season. The following comparison matrix of opportunity group (Table 4) reveal that large scale employment generations for the locals as well as for the migrant labourers from the neighboring districts (O6, importance degrees value: 0.29), expansion of agricultural markets and resulted escalation in demand (O3, value: 0.29) and extension of agro based and food processing (like potato powder, potato chips etc) industries (O1, value: 0.137) are the most

prioritized opportunities among the seven opportunity factors.

Among the threat factors, the fluctuation in economic profitability (T2, importance degree value: 0.27), occurrences of crop failures (T1, value: 0.22) and depletion of ground water level (T4, value: 0.17) due to excessive irrigation in post-monsoon seasons are found to be the most alarming issues in the district (Table 5). The potato-growers of the district experience oscillation of loss and profit from potato, depending upon the production scale and profit margin, which is adjusted in the share of land under potato for the next year and control the production

volume. The year of higher production is obviously linked to increase in area under potato and resultant fall in price. The farmers experiencing loss in the economy reduce area under potato in the next year leading to reduction in production which causes rise in the price of the crop. It has been found that in every alternative year, the area under potato has shown a trend of rise and fall as in Figure 2 (District Statistical Handbook, 1990-91 - 2013-14; Ghosh, 2017).
The small and marginal farmers are most vulnerable to the frequent oscillation in economic profitability related to the fluctuation in the productivity, expenditure,

market price and profits. In addition, recent changes in climatic phenomena with decreasing length of winter period hinder the natural growth and productivity of potato in the district (Ghosh, 2017). Besides, the extreme but infrequent climatic hazards lead towards prevalence of biotic hazards (fungal, viral and bacterial diseases) and often lead towards crop failure. Such climatic irregularities (winter rains and hailstorm, humidity and temperature fluctuation etc.) put extensive effect on the production volume. For example, winter rainfall in 2005- 06, hailstorm in 2006-07, and prevalence of pest diseases in 2008-09, have immensely damaged the potato cultivation across the district (Hatial and Mondal, 2015). Such climatic extremities, irregularities and the associated biotic hazards lead to massive crop failures where the small and marginal farmer are severely affected owing to poverty and lesser adaptive capacity to cope with the situation.

The analysis of the respondents' opinions has revealed that the second strategy i.e. SO2 has got the highest appreciation from the respondents (nearly 90%). The strategy number three (SO3) has been supported by 82% of the respondents, while SO1 has only 78% support from the stakeholders of the potato farming system in the study area (Fig. 3).

of potato in household level for consumption in daily basis which assumes that the internal demand is expected to be in an inflated state. Recent rapid booming of urbanization in the eastern margin of the district along the right bank of the river Hugli and consequent increment in number of census towns during the last decade (Siddique and Mukherjee, 2017) has enhanced the demand. Besides, the increasing commercial demand in newly flourished food-processing units in and around the district has enhanced the demand side. As the area under review is located close to the Kolkata metropolitan city and Haora, the area experiences a steady demand of potato. In addition, potatoes from this belt have been exported to the neighboring states also. Such escalated demands promises greater possibility of prosperity in production in near or distant future to meet the growing demands. Concurrently, the crop has huge potentiality to cope up with the increased food demand and poverty alleviation. The need for assured food security to the ever growing population with limited availability of cultivable land has necessitated spatio- temporal intensification of cropping. As a short duration cash crop, potato has easily accustomed with the existing cropping system and shows notable capability of adjustment with the changes in the system. Moreover, the crop has revealed its greater suitability in the varied agro-climatic conditions of the district. According to the Comprehensive District Agricultural Plan (CDAP) Report (2012) and Agricultural Technology Management Agency (ATMA) Report (2005-06) the district can be divided into three distinct agro-ecological situation zones (AES), of which AES I and II, covering nearly 83.77% of the district's area, are extensively suitable for potato cultivation. Almost 65.04% area under these two zones has already been used for potato farming. Even 21.62% area in AES III where monoculture

has been practiced, is also engaged in potato cultivation (Ghosh, 2017). In a nutshell, it may be said that the physical environment of the district is not a barrier; rather it provides an extensive field for potato farming.

The Table 3 has shown the comparison matrix of weakness aspects of the system. The analysis reveals that increasing expenditure costs and decreasing profit (W3, importance degree value: 0.25) are the most dominating weaknesses of the persisting agricultural system of the area. The cost of each component of agricultural input like chemical fertilizers, pesticides, labour, HYV seeds etc. along with transportation, irrigation, cold storage facilities has conspicuously increased over the past years. Field survey has revealed that the cost of chemical fertilizers and pesticides has been increased nearly 136.45% and 41.67% respectively from 2000 to 2019. Similarly, the cost of HYV seeds is risen about 68.75%, labour cost is increased nearly 160%, and irrigation cost is enhanced up to 200% during the stipulated phase. Overall, the estimated production cost per hectare area has been incremented to 111% during past twenty years. On an average, the irrigation cost comprises almost 9.47% of the total cost in 2019-20. As a rabi crop, potato is generally cultivated in the district during the dry months of the winter (October- February). Naturally, potato cultivation has to depend upon irrigation water. Therefore, huge amount of ground water has been extensively extracted through deep, shallow or submersible pumps for irrigation purpose, which has alarmingly reduced the level of ground water table. The rising production expenditure naturally reduces the profit of the producers. Most notable fact is that, the economic return from the field has failed to keep pace with mounting production cost over the past years. Even in a number of cases, the farmers have failed to recover their
Benojir Yasmin, Arindam Roy, Mehedi Hasan Mandal and Giyasuddin Siddique
Table 3: Comparison matrix of weakness group

Weaknesses (W)|W1|W2|W3|W4|W5|W6|W7|W8|Importance Degree W1 Climatic variability|1.00|2.00|0.33|0.50|1.00|4.00|3.00|3.00|0.15 W2 Prevalence of biotic hazards|0.50|1.00|0.50|1.00|0.50|3.00|2.00|3.00|0.12 W3 Increasing expenditure costs and decreasing profit|3.00|2.00|1.00|2.00|2.00|3.00|3.00|4.00|0.25 W4 Economically weak small and marginal farmers|2.00|1.00|0.50|1.00|0.33|3.00|2.00|2.00|0.13 W5 Irregularities in market and dominance of the middlemen|1.00|2.00|0.50|3.00|1.00|3.00|1.00|3.00|0.17 W6 Lack of infrastructural facilities|0.25|0.33|0.33|0.33|0.33|1.00|0.33|1.00|0.05 W7 Shortage of capital and growing dependency on bank and money lenders|0.33|0.50|0.33|0.50|1.00|3.00|1.00|2.00|0.09 W8 The absence of proper government policy and administrative control|0.33|0.33|0.25|0.50|0.33|1.00|0.50|1.00|0.05 CR = 0.05|||||||||

investments. Under such circumstances, the small and marginal farmers have become most vulnerable due to their incapability to combat the economic loss and sustain their livelihoods properly. They prefer loans from local money lenders [Mahajans] with high rate of interest (ranging from 10% to 15% per month during 2019-2020) to compensate for the loss and further beginning of cultivation and ultimately get trapped in debt. Thus the profit from the field of the potato growers gradually decreases which acts as a major economic constraints for potato cultivation.

Apart from this, the irregularities in market owing to the tricks of the middlemen (W5, value: 0.167), and climatic variability (W1, value: 0.15) are found to be another two most influencing internal weakness factors. The artificial crisis of potato in the market are often created by the middlemen (forias, aratdars) and the distributors for pocketing the benefit of inflated price where the economically weak small and marginal farmers are forced to sell the product at lower prices just after harvest and the middlemen multiply their profit each year. Due to the economic insolvency, the small and marginal
farmers often sell the crop from the field at a cheap cost in order to save the storage cost as the harvested products are non- preservable, easily rotten and damaged by diverse diseases.

The labour intensive nature of farming in the area with less use of modern machineries generates a huge demand of skilled and unskilled labourers in different stages from field preparation to marketing of potato. The greater potentialities of major agro-based industrial sector may create a wide scope of employment opportunities in near future. A bulk of poor fellow from the nearby rural areas or even from the neighboring districts migrate as agricultural labourers towards the area during harvesting season. The following comparison matrix of opportunity group (Table 4) reveal that large scale employment generations for the locals as well as for the migrant labourers from the neighboring districts (O6, importance degrees value: 0.29), expansion of agricultural markets and resulted escalation in demand (O3, value: 0.29) and extension of agro based and food processing (like potato powder, potato chips etc) industries (O1, value: 0.137) are the most
Table 4: Comparison matrix of opportunity group

Opportunities (O)|O1|O2|O3|O4|05|O6|O7|Importance Degrees 01 Extension of agro based and food processing industries|1.00|3.00|0.30|2.00|2.00|0.00|2.00|0.14 O2 Cultivation of different varieties of potato|3.00|1.00|0.30|0.00|0.50|0.00|2.00|0.07 Q3 Expansion of agricultural markets and escalation in demand|3.00|4.00|1.00|3.00|3.00|1.00|2.00|0.23 O4 Implementation of suitable policies regarding storing (like Kishan Mandi), marketing and trading|0.50|3.00|0.30|1.00|3.00|0.00|0.50|0.11 O5 Improvement in infrastructural facilities|0.50|2.00|0.30|0.00|1.00|0.00|0.50|0.07 O6 Large scale employment generations for the locals and for the migrant labourers|3.00|3.00|2.00|3.00|3.00|1.00|3.00|0.29 O7 Establishment and efficient management of large scale farms and participatory management system|0.50|1.00|0.50|2.00|2.00|0.00|1.00|0.10 CR = 0.09|CR = 0.09|CR = 0.09|CR = 0.09|CR = 0.09|CR = 0.09|CR = 0.09|CR = 0.09|CR = 0.09
prioritized opportunities among the seven opportunity factors.

Among the threat factors, the fluctuation in economic profitability (T2, importance degree value: 0.27), occurrences of crop failures (T1, value: 0.22) and depletion of ground water level (T4, value: 0.17) due to excessive irrigation in post-monsoon seasons are found to be the most alarming issues in the district (Table 5). The potato-growers of the district experience oscillation of loss and profit from potato, depending upon the production scale and profit margin, which is adjusted in the share of land under potato for the next year and control the production

volume. The year of higher production is obviously linked to increase in area under potato and resultant fall in price. The farmers experiencing loss in the economy reduce area under potato in the next year leading to reduction in production which causes rise in the price of the crop. It has been found that in every alternative year, the area under potato has shown a trend of rise and fall as in Figure 2 (District Statistical Handbook, 1990-91 - 2013-14; Ghosh, 2017).
The small and marginal farmers are most vulnerable to the frequent oscillation in economic profitability related to the fluctuation in the productivity, expenditure,
Table 5: Comparison matrix of threat group

market price and profits. In addition, recent changes in climatic phenomena with decreasing length of winter period hinder the natural growth and productivity of potato in the district (Ghosh, 2017). Besides, the extreme but infrequent climatic hazards lead towards prevalence of biotic hazards (fungal, viral and bacterial diseases) and often lead towards crop failure. Such climatic irregularities (winter rains and hailstorm, humidity and temperature fluctuation etc.) put extensive effect on the production volume. For example, winter rainfall in 2005- 06, hailstorm in 2006-07, and prevalence of pest diseases in 2008-09, have immensely damaged the potato cultivation across the district (Hatial and Mondal, 2015). Such climatic extremities, irregularities and the associated biotic hazards lead to massive crop failures where the small and marginal farmer are severely affected owing to poverty and lesser adaptive capacity to cope with the situation.

The ranks of the other considerable factors according to overall priority from each SWOT groups are as follows: large scale employment generations for the locals as well as the migrant labourers (0.10) from opportunity groups, increasing expenditure costs and decreasing profit (0.04) from weakness group, and fluctuation in economic profitability (0.03) from threat groups respectively.

pointed out the following strategies for the prosperity of potato cultivation of the district under review:
SO1: The favorable agro-climatic condition (S1) leads to production of large varieties of potatoes, which helps expand the

<list>agricultural markets and further escalation in demand (O3). 
SO2: Year round stability in demand (S5) will help sustain, rather expand the production scale, which will create large scale employment opportunity for the</list>
locals as well as for the migrant labourers from the neighboring districts (O6).
SO3: High cropping intensity with high production rate (S2) which will help flourish agro based and food processing industries (O1) in the area like potato powder, potato chips etc.

The analysis of the respondents' opinions has revealed that the second strategy i.e. SO2 has got the highest appreciation from the respondents (nearly 90%). The strategy number three (SO3) has been supported by 82% of the respondents, while SO1 has only 78% support from the stakeholders of the potato farming system in the study area (Fig. 3).

The demand of potato as cheap source of starch has every prospect to sustain long rather will increase with its use as different varieties of food items. <list>agricultural markets and further escalation in demand (O3).

The analysis of the respondents' opinions has revealed that the second strategy i.e. SO2 has got the highest appreciation from the respondents (nearly 90%). The strategy number three (SO3) has been supported by 82% of the respondents, while SO1 has only 78% support from the stakeholders of the potato farming system in the study area (Fig. 3).
18: <header>Analysis of the SWOT factors </header>

The Comparison Matrix of strength factors (Table 2) reveals that permanence in demand round the year (S5, importance degree value: 0.25) is the highest strength of potato production in the studied district followed by favorable agro-climatic conditions (S1, value: 0.23), and high cropping intensity and production rate (S2, value: 0.18). The demand of potato as cheap source of starch has every prospect to sustain long rather will increase with its use as different varieties of food items. The usage of potato in traditional Bengali diet is highly appreciated due to its taste, good nutrition value and affordable price. Naturally, there is a consistent demand
Table 2: Comparison matrix of strength group

Strengths (S)|S1|S2|S3|S4|S5|S6|S7|Importance Degrees S1 Favorable agro-climatic conditions|1.00|3.00|3.00|2.00|0.33|2.00|5.00|0.23 S2 High cropping intensity and production rate|0.33|1.00|2.00|2.00|1.00|3.00|4.00|0.18 S3 Long experience of potato cultivators (more than 40 years)|0.33|0.50|1.00|1.00|0.50|3.00|3.00|0.12 S4 Short duration crop that fits well within the prevailing cropping system|0.50|0.50|1.00|1.00|0.50|2.00|3.00|0.11 S5 Continuity in demand throughout the year|3.00|1.00|2.00|2.00|1.00|3.00|4.00|0.25 S6 Availability of local labour forces|0.50|0.33|0.33|0.50|0.33|1.00|4.00|0.08 S7 Farmer's willingness and massive engagement|0.20|0.25|0.33|0.33|0.25|0.25|1.00|0.04 C.R = 0.07||||||||

of potato in household level for consumption in daily basis which assumes that the internal demand is expected to be in an inflated state. Recent rapid booming of urbanization in the eastern margin of the district along the right bank of the river Hugli and consequent increment in number of census towns during the last decade (Siddique and Mukherjee, 2017) has enhanced the demand. Besides, the increasing commercial demand in newly flourished food-processing units in and around the district has enhanced the demand side. As the area under review is located close to the Kolkata metropolitan city and Haora, the area experiences a steady demand of potato. In addition, potatoes from this belt have been exported to the neighboring states also. Such escalated demands promises greater possibility of prosperity in production in near or distant future to meet the growing demands. Concurrently, the crop has huge potentiality to cope up with the increased food demand and poverty alleviation. The need for assured food security to the ever growing population with limited availability of cultivable land has necessitated spatio- temporal intensification of cropping. As a short duration cash crop, potato has easily accustomed with the existing cropping system and shows notable capability of adjustment with the changes in the system. Moreover, the crop has revealed its greater suitability in the varied agro-climatic conditions of the district. According to the Comprehensive District Agricultural Plan (CDAP) Report (2012) and Agricultural Technology Management Agency (ATMA) Report (2005-06) the district can be divided into three distinct agro-ecological situation zones (AES), of which AES I and II, covering nearly 83.77% of the district's area, are extensively suitable for potato cultivation. Almost 65.04% area under these two zones has already been used for potato farming. Even 21.62% area in AES III where monoculture

has been practiced, is also engaged in potato cultivation (Ghosh, 2017). In a nutshell, it may be said that the physical environment of the district is not a barrier; rather it provides an extensive field for potato farming.

The Table 3 has shown the comparison matrix of weakness aspects of the system. The analysis reveals that increasing expenditure costs and decreasing profit (W3, importance degree value: 0.25) are the most dominating weaknesses of the persisting agricultural system of the area. The cost of each component of agricultural input like chemical fertilizers, pesticides, labour, HYV seeds etc. along with transportation, irrigation, cold storage facilities has conspicuously increased over the past years. Field survey has revealed that the cost of chemical fertilizers and pesticides has been increased nearly 136.45% and 41.67% respectively from 2000 to 2019. Similarly, the cost of HYV seeds is risen about 68.75%, labour cost is increased nearly 160%, and irrigation cost is enhanced up to 200% during the stipulated phase. Overall, the estimated production cost per hectare area has been incremented to 111% during past twenty years. On an average, the irrigation cost comprises almost 9.47% of the total cost in 2019-20. As a rabi crop, potato is generally cultivated in the district during the dry months of the winter (October- February). Naturally, potato cultivation has to depend upon irrigation water. Therefore, huge amount of ground water has been extensively extracted through deep, shallow or submersible pumps for irrigation purpose, which has alarmingly reduced the level of ground water table. The rising production expenditure naturally reduces the profit of the producers. Most notable fact is that, the economic return from the field has failed to keep pace with mounting production cost over the past years. Even in a number of cases, the farmers have failed to recover their
Benojir Yasmin, Arindam Roy, Mehedi Hasan Mandal and Giyasuddin Siddique
Table 3: Comparison matrix of weakness group

Weaknesses (W)|W1|W2|W3|W4|W5|W6|W7|W8|Importance Degree W1 Climatic variability|1.00|2.00|0.33|0.50|1.00|4.00|3.00|3.00|0.15 W2 Prevalence of biotic hazards|0.50|1.00|0.50|1.00|0.50|3.00|2.00|3.00|0.12 W3 Increasing expenditure costs and decreasing profit|3.00|2.00|1.00|2.00|2.00|3.00|3.00|4.00|0.25 W4 Economically weak small and marginal farmers|2.00|1.00|0.50|1.00|0.33|3.00|2.00|2.00|0.13 W5 Irregularities in market and dominance of the middlemen|1.00|2.00|0.50|3.00|1.00|3.00|1.00|3.00|0.17 W6 Lack of infrastructural facilities|0.25|0.33|0.33|0.33|0.33|1.00|0.33|1.00|0.05 W7 Shortage of capital and growing dependency on bank and money lenders|0.33|0.50|0.33|0.50|1.00|3.00|1.00|2.00|0.09 W8 The absence of proper government policy and administrative control|0.33|0.33|0.25|0.50|0.33|1.00|0.50|1.00|0.05 CR = 0.05|||||||||

investments. Under such circumstances, the small and marginal farmers have become most vulnerable due to their incapability to combat the economic loss and sustain their livelihoods properly. They prefer loans from local money lenders [Mahajans] with high rate of interest (ranging from 10% to 15% per month during 2019-2020) to compensate for the loss and further beginning of cultivation and ultimately get trapped in debt. Thus the profit from the field of the potato growers gradually decreases which acts as a major economic constraints for potato cultivation.

Apart from this, the irregularities in market owing to the tricks of the middlemen (W5, value: 0.167), and climatic variability (W1, value: 0.15) are found to be another two most influencing internal weakness factors. The artificial crisis of potato in the market are often created by the middlemen (forias, aratdars) and the distributors for pocketing the benefit of inflated price where the economically weak small and marginal farmers are forced to sell the product at lower prices just after harvest and the middlemen multiply their profit each year. Due to the economic insolvency, the small and marginal
farmers often sell the crop from the field at a cheap cost in order to save the storage cost as the harvested products are non- preservable, easily rotten and damaged by diverse diseases.

The labour intensive nature of farming in the area with less use of modern machineries generates a huge demand of skilled and unskilled labourers in different stages from field preparation to marketing of potato. The greater potentialities of major agro-based industrial sector may create a wide scope of employment opportunities in near future. A bulk of poor fellow from the nearby rural areas or even from the neighboring districts migrate as agricultural labourers towards the area during harvesting season. The following comparison matrix of opportunity group (Table 4) reveal that large scale employment generations for the locals as well as for the migrant labourers from the neighboring districts (O6, importance degrees value: 0.29), expansion of agricultural markets and resulted escalation in demand (O3, value: 0.29) and extension of agro based and food processing (like potato powder, potato chips etc) industries (O1, value: 0.137) are the most

prioritized opportunities among the seven opportunity factors.

Among the threat factors, the fluctuation in economic profitability (T2, importance degree value: 0.27), occurrences of crop failures (T1, value: 0.22) and depletion of ground water level (T4, value: 0.17) due to excessive irrigation in post-monsoon seasons are found to be the most alarming issues in the district (Table 5). The potato-growers of the district experience oscillation of loss and profit from potato, depending upon the production scale and profit margin, which is adjusted in the share of land under potato for the next year and control the production

volume. The year of higher production is obviously linked to increase in area under potato and resultant fall in price. The farmers experiencing loss in the economy reduce area under potato in the next year leading to reduction in production which causes rise in the price of the crop. It has been found that in every alternative year, the area under potato has shown a trend of rise and fall as in Figure 2 (District Statistical Handbook, 1990-91 - 2013-14; Ghosh, 2017).
The small and marginal farmers are most vulnerable to the frequent oscillation in economic profitability related to the fluctuation in the productivity, expenditure,

market price and profits. In addition, recent changes in climatic phenomena with decreasing length of winter period hinder the natural growth and productivity of potato in the district (Ghosh, 2017). Besides, the extreme but infrequent climatic hazards lead towards prevalence of biotic hazards (fungal, viral and bacterial diseases) and often lead towards crop failure. Such climatic irregularities (winter rains and hailstorm, humidity and temperature fluctuation etc.) put extensive effect on the production volume. For example, winter rainfall in 2005- 06, hailstorm in 2006-07, and prevalence of pest diseases in 2008-09, have immensely damaged the potato cultivation across the district (Hatial and Mondal, 2015). Such climatic extremities, irregularities and the associated biotic hazards lead to massive crop failures where the small and marginal farmer are severely affected owing to poverty and lesser adaptive capacity to cope with the situation.

Apart from this, the irregularities in market owing to the tricks of the middlemen (W5, value: 0.167), and climatic variability (W1, value: 0.15) are found to be another two most influencing internal weakness factors. The artificial crisis of potato in the market are often created by the middlemen (forias, aratdars) and the distributors for pocketing the benefit of inflated price where the economically weak small and marginal farmers are forced to sell the product at lower prices just after harvest and the middlemen multiply their profit each year. Due to the economic insolvency, the small and marginal
farmers often sell the crop from the field at a cheap cost in order to save the storage cost as the harvested products are non- preservable, easily rotten and damaged by diverse diseases.

The labour intensive nature of farming in the area with less use of modern machineries generates a huge demand of skilled and unskilled labourers in different stages from field preparation to marketing of potato. The greater potentialities of major agro-based industrial sector may create a wide scope of employment opportunities in near future. A bulk of poor fellow from the nearby rural areas or even from the neighboring districts migrate as agricultural labourers towards the area during harvesting season. The following comparison matrix of opportunity group (Table 4) reveal that large scale employment generations for the locals as well as for the migrant labourers from the neighboring districts (O6, importance degrees value: 0.29), expansion of agricultural markets and resulted escalation in demand (O3, value: 0.29) and extension of agro based and food processing (like potato powder, potato chips etc) industries (O1, value: 0.137) are the most

prioritized opportunities among the seven opportunity factors.

Among the threat factors, the fluctuation in economic profitability (T2, importance degree value: 0.27), occurrences of crop failures (T1, value: 0.22) and depletion of ground water level (T4, value: 0.17) due to excessive irrigation in post-monsoon seasons are found to be the most alarming issues in the district (Table 5). The potato-growers of the district experience oscillation of loss and profit from potato, depending upon the production scale and profit margin, which is adjusted in the share of land under potato for the next year and control the production

volume. The year of higher production is obviously linked to increase in area under potato and resultant fall in price. The farmers experiencing loss in the economy reduce area under potato in the next year leading to reduction in production which causes rise in the price of the crop. It has been found that in every alternative year, the area under potato has shown a trend of rise and fall as in Figure 2 (District Statistical Handbook, 1990-91 - 2013-14; Ghosh, 2017).
The small and marginal farmers are most vulnerable to the frequent oscillation in economic profitability related to the fluctuation in the productivity, expenditure,

market price and profits. In addition, recent changes in climatic phenomena with decreasing length of winter period hinder the natural growth and productivity of potato in the district (Ghosh, 2017). Besides, the extreme but infrequent climatic hazards lead towards prevalence of biotic hazards (fungal, viral and bacterial diseases) and often lead towards crop failure. Such climatic irregularities (winter rains and hailstorm, humidity and temperature fluctuation etc.) put extensive effect on the production volume. For example, winter rainfall in 2005- 06, hailstorm in 2006-07, and prevalence of pest diseases in 2008-09, have immensely damaged the potato cultivation across the district (Hatial and Mondal, 2015). Such climatic extremities, irregularities and the associated biotic hazards lead to massive crop failures where the small and marginal farmer are severely affected owing to poverty and lesser adaptive capacity to cope with the situation.

20: <header>Analysis of the SWOT factors </header>

The Comparison Matrix of strength factors (Table 2) reveals that permanence in demand round the year (S5, importance degree value: 0.25) is the highest strength of potato production in the studied district followed by favorable agro-climatic conditions (S1, value: 0.23), and high cropping intensity and production rate (S2, value: 0.18). The demand of potato as cheap source of starch has every prospect to sustain long rather will increase with its use as different varieties of food items. The usage of potato in traditional Bengali diet is highly appreciated due to its taste, good nutrition value and affordable price. Naturally, there is a consistent demand
Table 2: Comparison matrix of strength group
